# Research Synthesis & Matching Mechanism

## Overview

This file defines the core "automatic thinking brain" of the biomed-research skill. It provides:
1. A matching mechanism that routes user queries to the correct reference files and modules
2. A synthesis protocol that combines multiple references into actionable research guidance
3. A research simulation engine that generates experiment designs from literature analysis

---

## Reference File Index & Matching Rules

| File | Content | When to Load |

### Matching Algorithm

```
INPUT: User query text
OUTPUT: Ordered list of reference files to consult

Step 1: Extract signal type keywords
  ECG / EKG / heart / cardiac / arrhythmia / QRS / AF → load ecg-methodology.md
  EEG / brain / BCI / motor imagery / sleep staging → load eeg-bci-methodology.md
  PPG / photoplethysmography / pulse / blood pressure → load signal-processing-foundations.md
  imaging / X-ray / CT / MRI / fMRI / pathology / WSI → load medical-imaging-methodology.md + database-api-guide.md (TIER 5)
  genomics / protein / single-cell / GWAS / RNA-seq / drug discovery → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md + database-api-guide.md (TIER 6)
  NLP / LLM / clinical text / NER / RAG / prompt → load clinical-nlp-llm-methodology.md
  ethics / fairness / bias / consent / privacy → load research-ethics-fairness.md
  reproducibility / Docker / model card / data sheet → load reproducibility-infrastructure.md
  Mendelian randomization / MR / causal genomics / colocalization / eQTL / pQTL → load causal-genomics-methodology.md
  CRISPR / gRNA / guide RNA / gene editing / base editor / prime editor → load crispr-design-methodology.md
  experimental design / power analysis / sample size / RCT design / randomization → load experimental-design-methodology.md
  epitope / vaccine / MHC / immunoinformatics / neoantigen → load immunoinformatics-methodology.md
  Hi-C / 3D genome / chromatin conformation / TAD / promoter-enhancer → load hi-c-3d-genome-methodology.md
  flow cytometry / FACS / CyTOF / immunophenotyping → load flow-cytometry-methodology.md
  m6A / RNA modification / epitranscriptomics / METTL3 / FTO → load epitranscriptomics-methodology.md
  liquid biopsy / ctDNA / CTC / exosome / cfDNA / MRD → load liquid-biopsy-methodology.md

Step 2: Extract method type keywords
  deep learning / CNN / RNN / Transformer / neural network → load deep-learning-bme.md
  GNN / graph neural / molecular graph / drug-target → load deep-learning-bme.md (GNN section)
  diffusion / generative / synthetic image / molecular generation → load deep-learning-bme.md (Diffusion section)
  distillation / compression / quantization / edge deployment → load deep-learning-bme.md (Distillation section)
  self-supervised / SSL / pretraining / contrastive / MAE / DINO → load deep-learning-bme.md (SSL section)
  federated / federated learning / multi-site / privacy-preserving → load deep-learning-bme.md (Federated Learning section)
  active learning / annotation efficiency / labeling cost → load deep-learning-bme.md (Active Learning section)
  uncertainty / calibration / MC Dropout / conformal / OOD → load deep-learning-bme.md (Uncertainty section)
  explainability / XAI / SHAP / GradCAM / interpretability → load deep-learning-bme.md (XAI section)
  CSP / ICA / wavelet / filtering / preprocessing → load signal-processing-foundations.md
  SVM / random forest / XGBoost / traditional ML → load deep-learning-bme.md (comparison section)
  causal / propensity / instrumental variable / DAG / counterfactual → load clinical-statistical-framework.md (Causal Inference section)
  Mendelian randomization / IVW / MR-Egger / weighted median / pleiotropy → load causal-genomics-methodology.md
  colocalization / COLOC / eCAVIAR / GWAS-to-function / eQTL colocalization → load causal-genomics-methodology.md
  gRNA design / off-target prediction / on-target scoring / CRISPR efficiency → load crispr-design-methodology.md
  base editing / prime editing / HDR / NHEJ / CRISPR delivery → load crispr-design-methodology.md
  CRISPR screen / pooled screen / arrayed screen / MAGeCK → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (CRISPR Screen section)
  power analysis / sample size / G*Power / effect size / Cohen's d → load experimental-design-methodology.md
  randomization / blinding / allocation concealment / factorial design → load experimental-design-methodology.md
  bias assessment / RoB2 / ROBINS-I / risk of bias / CONSORT / STROBE / PRISMA → load experimental-design-methodology.md
  epitope prediction / B-cell epitope / T-cell epitope / NetMHCpan / MHC binding → load immunoinformatics-methodology.md
  vaccine design / immunogen / adjuvant / mRNA vaccine / reverse vaccinology → load immunoinformatics-methodology.md
  neoantigen / cancer vaccine / personalized vaccine / tumor antigen → load immunoinformatics-methodology.md
  Hi-C / chromatin conformation / TAD / loop calling / compartment → load hi-c-3d-genome-methodology.md
  3D genome / promoter-enhancer / chromatin interaction / Juicebox → load hi-c-3d-genome-methodology.md
  flow cytometry panel / fluorochrome / compensation / gating strategy → load flow-cytometry-methodology.md
  CyTOF / mass cytometry / spectral flow / high-dimensional cytometry / FlowSOM / Phenograph → load flow-cytometry-methodology.md
  m6A / MeRIP-seq / miCLIP / writer / reader / eraser → load epitranscriptomics-methodology.md
  RNA modification / m5C / pseudouridine / epitranscriptome → load epitranscriptomics-methodology.md
  ctDNA / CTC / cfDNA / liquid biopsy / fragmentomics → load liquid-biopsy-methodology.md
  MRD / minimal residual disease / early cancer detection / NIPT → load liquid-biopsy-methodology.md

Step 2.5: Extract validation/clinical keywords (NEW — bridges domain files to clinical)
  clinical validation / FDA / regulatory / CE mark → load clinical-statistical-framework.md
  ICD / diagnosis / billing codes / label quality → load clinical-statistical-framework.md (Label Quality section)
  trial / RCT / prospective / retrospective → load clinical-statistical-framework.md
  real-world data / claims / SEER / Medicare / cohort → load database-api-guide.md (TIER 10-12)
  power / sample size / non-inferiority → load clinical-statistical-framework.md (Sample Size section)
  survival / Kaplan-Meier / Cox / hazard ratio / competing risk → load clinical-statistical-framework.md (Survival Analysis section)
  missing data / imputation / MICE / MCAR / MAR → load clinical-statistical-framework.md (Missing Data section)
  accountability / TRUST / data sovereignty / CARE / environmental → load research-ethics-fairness.md (Accountability section)
  red-team / LLM safety / hallucination risk → load research-ethics-fairness.md (LLM Ethics section)
  drift / monitoring / MLOps / model versioning / deployment → load reproducibility-infrastructure.md (MLOps section)
  proteomics / metabolomics / mass spectrometry / PLS-DA → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (Proteomics section)
  epigenomics / ATAC-seq / methylation / chromatin → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (Epigenomics section)
  PPI / drug-target interaction / DTI / binding prediction → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (DTI section)
  auscultation / lung sound / breath sound / stethoscope / heart sound / murmur → load signal-processing-foundations.md (Auscultation section)
  voice / speech biomarker / cough / Parkinson voice → load signal-processing-foundations.md (Voice section)
  ultrasound RF / beamforming / elastography / shear wave → load signal-processing-foundations.md (Ultrasound section)
  foundation model / time-series foundation / PatchTST / Chronos / biosignal pretraining → load signal-processing-foundations.md (Foundation Model section)
  wearable / on-device / edge / quantization / TFLite / CMSIS / low-power → load signal-processing-foundations.md (Wearable Deployment section)
  pathology / WSI / stain / MIL / slide-level / patching → load medical-imaging-methodology.md (Digital Pathology section)
  endoscopy / colonoscopy / polyp / CADe / CADx → load medical-imaging-methodology.md (Endoscopy section)
  retinal / fundus / ophthalmology / DR screening / glaucoma → load medical-imaging-methodology.md (Ophthalmology section)
  report generation / radiology report / hallucination / RadGraph / CheXpert → load medical-imaging-methodology.md (Report Generation section)
  ICD / coding / ICD-10 / CPT / SNOMED / billing → load clinical-nlp-llm-methodology.md (Clinical Coding section)
  drug NLP / ADE / DDI / pharmacovigilance / adverse event → load clinical-nlp-llm-methodology.md (Drug NLP section)
  trial matching / eligibility / ClinicalTrials / recruitment → load clinical-nlp-llm-methodology.md (Trial Matching section)
  multilingual / Chinese NLP / cross-lingual / code-switching → load clinical-nlp-llm-methodology.md (Multilingual section)

  bone age / BoneXpert / Greulich-Pyle / Tanner-Whitehouse / pediatric bone → load medical-imaging-methodology.md (Musculoskeletal AI section)
  osteoarthritis / KL grade / joint space narrowing / cartilage loss / knee X-ray → load medical-imaging-methodology.md (Musculoskeletal AI section)
  spine / Cobb angle / disc degeneration / spinal stenosis / spondylolisthesis → load medical-imaging-methodology.md (Musculoskeletal AI section)
  PPG / photoplethysmography / respiratory rate from PPG / cuffless blood pressure / pulse transit time / PAT → load signal-processing-foundations.md (Advanced PPG section)
  AF detection PPG / Apple Heart Study / irregular rhythm / PPG arrhythmia / consumer wearable AF → load signal-processing-foundations.md (Advanced PPG section)
  skin pigmentation bias / pulse oximetry bias / SpO2 accuracy / dark skin → load signal-processing-foundations.md (Advanced PPG section)
  BCG / ballistocardiography / force plate / mattress sensor / J-wave / cardiac output estimation → load signal-processing-foundations.md (BCG/SCG section)
  SCG / seismocardiography / accelerometer cardiac / systolic time intervals / PEP / LVET / aortic valve → load signal-processing-foundations.md (BCG/SCG section)
  adversarial robustness / distribution shift / scanner shift / subgroup robustness / FGSM / PGD / corruption error → load deep-learning-bme.md (Adversarial Robustness section)
  continual learning / lifelong learning / catastrophic forgetting / EWC / replay / PCCP / adaptive algorithm / FDA adaptive → load deep-learning-bme.md (Continual Learning section)
  domain adaptation / DANN / test-time adaptation / style transfer / prompt tuning / fine-tune target / BN adaptation → load deep-learning-bme.md (Domain Adaptation section)
  implementation science / deployment gap / workflow integration / shadow mode / phased rollout / SMART on FHIR / alert fatigue mitigation → load reproducibility-infrastructure.md (AI Deployment section)
  ROI measurement / maintenance gap / automation bias / clinician adoption / deployment failure → load reproducibility-infrastructure.md (AI Deployment section)
  ⚠️ This step ensures clinical validation needs are detected EARLY, not just in Step 4

Step 2.6: Extract OpenClaw-enhanced domain keywords (NEW — drug/oncology/trial/documentation routing)
  drug repurposing / repositioning / drug discovery / target validation / druggability → load drug-discovery-pharmacology-methodology.md
  DDI / drug-drug interaction / CYP450 / CYP3A4 / CYP2D6 / CYP2C19 / P-gp / transporter → load drug-discovery-pharmacology-methodology.md (DDI section)
  pharmacovigilance / signal detection / PRR / ROR / FAERS / adverse event / Naranjo → load drug-discovery-pharmacology-methodology.md (Pharmacovigilance section)
  ADMET / absorption / distribution / metabolism / excretion / toxicity / pkCSM → load drug-discovery-pharmacology-methodology.md (ADMET section)
  precision oncology / actionable mutation / OncoKB / CIViC / cancer variant / targeted therapy → load precision-oncology-immunotherapy-methodology.md
  immunotherapy / ICI / checkpoint inhibitor / PD-L1 / PD-1 / CTLA-4 / TMB / MSI → load precision-oncology-immunotherapy-methodology.md
  antibody / humanization / developability / affinity maturation / CDR / Fc engineering → load precision-oncology-immunotherapy-methodology.md (Antibody section)
  TCR / BCR / immune repertoire / clonotype / CDR3 / V(D)J / TIL → load precision-oncology-immunotherapy-methodology.md (Immune Repertoire section)
  RFdiffusion / ProteinMPNN / de novo protein / binder design / protein therapeutic → load precision-oncology-immunotherapy-methodology.md (Protein Design section)
  network pharmacology / PPI network / STRING / drug-target-disease → load network-pharmacology-systems-biology.md
  GO enrichment / KEGG / GSEA / GSVA / pathway analysis / gene set → load network-pharmacology-systems-biology.md (Enrichment section)
  GRN / gene regulatory network / GENIE3 / SCENIC / transcription factor → load network-pharmacology-systems-biology.md (GRN section)
  systems biology / PBPK / PK-PD / ODE model / agent-based → load network-pharmacology-systems-biology.md (Systems Biology section)
  disease subtype / molecular subtype / iCluster / SNF / MOFA / multi-omics integration → load network-pharmacology-systems-biology.md (Disease Characterization section)
  clinical trial / trial design / protocol / Phase I/II/III / adaptive design → load clinical-trial-design-methodology.md
  platform trial / basket trial / umbrella trial / RECOVERY / REMAP-CAP / master protocol → load clinical-trial-design-methodology.md (Adaptive Design section)
  trial matching / eligibility / enrollment / patient-to-trial / ClinicalTrials.gov matching → load clinical-trial-design-methodology.md (Trial Matching section)
  feasibility / enrollment projection / site activation / competitive landscape → load clinical-trial-design-methodology.md (Feasibility section)
  clinical documentation / CARE / SOAP / H&P / discharge summary / ICH-E3 / CSR → load clinical-documentation-decision-support.md
  CDS / clinical decision support / treatment recommendation / biomarker integration → load clinical-documentation-decision-support.md (CDS section)
  GRADE / evidence grading / guideline / NICE / NCCN / AHA / ACC / ADA / CPIC / ESMO → load clinical-documentation-decision-support.md (Guidelines section)
  FHIR / HL7 / API development / resource type / Observation / Patient → load clinical-documentation-decision-support.md (FHIR section)
  health literacy / patient communication / readability / simplify / patient-facing → load clinical-documentation-decision-support.md (Health Literacy section)
  entity extraction / negation detection / dosage extraction / medical NER → load clinical-documentation-decision-support.md (Entity Extraction section)
  PRS / polygenic risk score / PRS-CS / LDpred2 / PRS-CSx / risk stratification → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (PRS section)
  ACMG / variant classification / PVS1 / BA1 / VUS / pathogenic / benign → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (ACMG section)
  fine-mapping / credible set / SuSiE / FINEMAP / COLOC / colocalization → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (Fine-Mapping section)
  SV / structural variant / CNV / copy number / GISTIC / ABSOLUTE / CNVkit → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (SV/CNV section)
  CRISPR screen / MAGeCK / sgRNA / knockout screen / essential gene → load genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md (CRISPR Screen section)

Step 3: Extract task type keywords
  reproduce / replicate / implement / code → load ecg-methodology.md OR eeg-bci-methodology.md (domain-specific) + deep-learning-bme.md
  verify / evidence / claim / prove → load clinical-statistical-framework.md
  compare / vs / difference → load domain-specific methodology file + clinical-statistical-framework.md
  dataset / data / PhysioNet → load physionet-datasets.md + database-api-guide.md
  topic / gap / idea / 选题 → load ALL relevant domain files

Step 4: Extract evaluation keywords
  clinical / validation / FDA / regulatory → load clinical-statistical-framework.md
  statistics / p-value / confidence / significance → load clinical-statistical-framework.md
  benchmark / SOTA / performance → load domain-specific methodology file

Step 5: Prioritize
  If multiple files match, prioritize by:
  1. Domain-specific methodology (ECG > EEG > Imaging > DL > Genomics > Clinical > Signal > Causal Genomics > CRISPR > Immunoinformatics > Hi-C > Flow Cytometry > Epitranscriptomics > Liquid Biopsy > Experimental Design > Dataset > DB)
  2. Task-specific (reproduction > verification > comparison > ideation)
  3. Load maximum 4 files per response (increased from 3 — cross-domain queries need more)
  4. ⚠️ BRIDGE RULE: If query spans 2+ domains (e.g., "ECG + clinical validation"), ALWAYS load:
     - Both domain methodology files
     - clinical-statistical-framework.md (as the bridge)
     - physionet-datasets.md (for data access)
     This takes priority over the 4-file limit — cross-domain queries need comprehensive coverage
  5. ⚠️ CROSS-DOMAIN SYNERGY RULE: For queries spanning genomics + causal inference, load:
     - causal-genomics-methodology.md + genomics-bioinformatics-methodology.md
     For queries spanning immunology + oncology, load:
     - immunoinformatics-methodology.md + precision-oncology-immunotherapy-methodology.md
     For queries spanning epigenomics + 3D genome, load:
     - epitranscriptomics-methodology.md + hi-c-3d-genome-methodology.md
     For queries spanning liquid biopsy + oncology, load:
     - liquid-biopsy-methodology.md + precision-oncology-immunotherapy-methodology.md
```

### Paper Type Classification (Run BEFORE Protocol A/B/C)

```
INPUT: Paper title, abstract, methods section
OUTPUT: Paper type classification

Step 1: Identify paper type
  → Original research (new method/model/dataset) → Use Protocol A
  → Review / Survey / Meta-analysis → Use Protocol D (below)
  → AI-assisted discovery (AI finds new knowledge/biomarkers/targets) → Use Protocol E (below)
  → Dataset / Benchmark paper → Use Protocol A with dataset-specific checks
  → Challenge / Competition paper → Use Protocol A with challenge-specific checks

Step 2: For Review/Survey papers (Protocol D):
  → DO NOT run Fatal Blocker Detector (not applicable)
  → Instead, evaluate with QUANTITATIVE scoring:
     a. COMPLETENESS (0-2): 0=Missing key papers; 1=Covers most but gaps; 2=Comprehensive
     b. BIAS (0-2): 0=Only positive results; 1=Some negative; 2=Balanced positive/negative
     c. TIMELINESS (0-2): 0=Outdated (>3yr old); 1=Mostly current; 2=Includes last 2yr SOTA
     d. SYNTHESIS (0-2): 0=Just a catalog; 1=Some analysis; 2=New insights/taxonomy/framework
     e. REPRODUCIBILITY (0-2): 0=Not actionable; 1=Partially actionable; 2=Full pipeline reproducible
  → Total score: /10
  → Rating: 0-3=Weak; 4-6=Moderate; 7-8=Good; 9-10=Excellent
  → Output: "This is a REVIEW paper. Score: X/10 [Rating]. Key gaps: [list]"

Step 3: For AI-assisted discovery papers (Protocol E):
  → Run Fatal Blocker Detector with MODIFIED criteria:
     FB-1: Is the training data for discovery available? (e.g., literature corpus, protein database)
     FB-2: Is the discovery code available?
     FB-EXTRA-1: Was the AI prediction experimentally validated? (wet lab, clinical, etc.)
     FB-EXTRA-2: Was the validation done by independent researchers?
     FB-EXTRA-3: What's the hit rate? (How many predictions were tested? How many confirmed?)
  → Key question: "Is this AI discovery or AI hypothesis generation?"
     → If hypothesis generation only → Evidence level VI (needs experimental validation)
     → If experimentally validated → Evidence level III-IV (depending on validation rigor)
  → Output: "This is an AI-DISCOVERY paper. Evidence level depends on experimental validation."

Step 4: For Challenge/Competition papers:
  → Check: Is the challenge metric standard? (AUROC, F1) Or custom? (challenge-specific score)
  → If custom metric → Flag: "⚠️ Challenge metric is not directly comparable to standard metrics"
  → Check: Is the hidden test set representative? Or biased toward specific conditions?
  → Check: Are the top solutions ensemble methods? (Common in competitions, impractical for deployment)
```

### Validation Scenario Representativeness Check

When a paper reports external validation, check if the validation scenario matches the deployment claim:

| Claim | Required Validation | Common Mismatch |
|-------|--------------------|-----------------| 
| "Detects AF in general population" | Validation on community cohort, not just cardiology patients | Validated only on cardioversion patients (extreme AF prevalence) |
| "Sepsis prediction for all ICU patients" | Multi-center, mixed ICU types | Validated only on surgical ICU or single-center |
| "Skin cancer screening for primary care" | Validation on primary care images | Validated on dermatology clinic images (higher prevalence, more typical lesions) |
| "Sleep staging for home monitoring" | Validation on home PSG or wearable EEG | Validated only on lab PSG (cleaner signals) |
| "ECG diagnosis in low-resource settings" | Validation on noisy, single-lead ECG | Validated on clean 12-lead hospital ECG |

**Rule**: If the validation population differs significantly from the claimed deployment population, flag: "⚠️ Validation scenario mismatch — model may not perform as claimed in target setting."

### Regulatory Pathway Quick Reference

When evaluating clinical AI papers for deployment potential:

| Regulatory Framework | Region | Key Requirements | Typical Timeline |
|---------------------|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| FDA 510(k) | US | Substantial equivalence to predicate device | 3-12 months |
| FDA De Novo | US | Novel device, no predicate; requires clinical evidence | 6-18 months |
| FDA PMA | US | High-risk; requires clinical trial data | 1-3 years |
| CE MDR | EU | Clinical evaluation + post-market surveillance | 6-18 months |
| SaMD (IMDRF) | International | Risk classification framework for software | Varies |

**Key FDA AI/ML-specific guidance**:
- FDA expects "Predetermined Change Control Plan" for adaptive algorithms
- Locking algorithm vs Adaptive algorithm: Locked is easier to clear
- Real-world performance monitoring is required post-market
- Reference: FDA "Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Software as a Medical Device" (2021+)

**Rule**: If a paper claims clinical deployment but has no regulatory pathway discussion, flag: "⚠️ No regulatory pathway discussed — clinical deployment is not imminent."

### Terminology Inflation Check

When a paper uses trendy terms (digital twin, foundation model, generalist AI, etc.), verify the claim matches the substance:

| Term | Required Characteristics | Common Misuse | Flag |
|------|------------------------|---------------|------|
| **Digital Twin** | Personalized + dynamic updating + bidirectional data flow + predictive | Static model without updating; one-time simulation; population-level model | "⚠️ Not a digital twin — missing [dynamic updating / bidirectional flow / personalization]" |
| **Foundation Model** | Pretrained on diverse data + adaptable to many downstream tasks | Single-task model; no pretraining; no downstream adaptation demonstrated | "⚠️ Not a foundation model — no evidence of multi-task generalization" |
| **Generalist AI** | Single model performing diverse tasks across modalities | Multi-task within single modality; separate models per task | "⚠️ Not generalist — limited to single modality/task type" |
| **Zero-shot** | No examples from target task/domain used | Uses examples from same domain; or "zero-shot" with task description that contains answer | "⚠️ Not truly zero-shot — [specify what makes it few-shot]" |
| **Clinically validated** | Prospective evaluation in clinical setting with patient outcomes | Retrospective evaluation only; no clinical deployment; only technical metrics | "⚠️ Not clinically validated — retrospective evaluation ≠ clinical validation" |

**Rule**: If >50% of papers in a subfield misuse a term, add a specific check for that term in the domain methodology file.

### Digital Twin Paper Evaluation Protocol

When evaluating papers claiming to create "digital twins" of patients/organs:

```
DIGITAL TWIN EVALUATION CHECKLIST (Based on NASEM 2024 Standards)
=================================================================

1. DEFINITION CHECK
   → Does it have a personalized model? (Not just population-level)
   → Does it have dynamic data updating? (Not just one-time calibration)
   → Does it have bidirectional data flow? (Patient ↔ Model, not just Patient → Model)
   → Does it support predictive decision-making? (Not just monitoring)

2. CLASSIFICATION (7-level, per NASEM)
   Level 1: Virtual patient cohort (population model) → NOT a digital twin
   Level 2: Generic digital model → NOT a digital twin
   Level 3: Personalized digital model → Partial digital twin
   Level 4: Personalized model for single decision → Partial digital twin
   Level 5: Digital shadow (1-way flow) → Partial digital twin
   Level 6: Human-in-the-loop digital twin → TRUE digital twin
   Level 7: Auto-updating digital twin → TRUE digital twin (rarest)

3. VALIDATION CHECK
   → VVUQ (Verification, Validation, Uncertainty Quantification): Is it reported?
   → Only 2% of current digital twin papers report VVUQ → Most are unvalidated
   → Is there prospective validation? (Most are retrospective only)

4. REPRODUCIBILITY
   → Is the simulation code available?
   → Is the patient data available? (Usually not → major reproduction barrier)
   → Can the model be retrained on new patients?
```

**Rule**: If a paper claims "digital twin" but is Level 1-3, flag: "⚠️ This is a [virtual cohort / generic model / personalized model], NOT a true digital twin per NASEM standards. Missing: [dynamic updating / bidirectional flow / predictive capability]."

---

## Research Synthesis Protocol

### Protocol A: Single-Paper Synthesis

When analyzing a single paper, synthesize from references as follows:

```
1. CLASSIFY the paper's domain (ECG / EEG / BCI / Clinical / Imaging / Tabular)
   → Load the corresponding methodology reference file

2. EXTRACT the paper's method using the domain-specific thinking protocol
   → ECG: Use ECG Automatic Thinking Protocol from ecg-methodology.md
   → EEG: Use EEG/BCI Automatic Thinking Protocol from eeg-bci-methodology.md
   → DL: Use DL Architecture Selection Framework from deep-learning-bme.md
   → Clinical: Use Statistical Rigor Checklist from clinical-statistical-framework.md

3. ⚡ FATAL BLOCKER IDENTIFICATION (MANDATORY — DO THIS BEFORE ANYTHING ELSE)
   → Run the Fatal Blocker Detector (see below)
   → If ANY blocker is 🔴 CRITICAL, output it as the FIRST thing in the response
   → A fatal blocker means: "This paper CANNOT be meaningfully reproduced/verified as-is"
   → Common fatal blockers:
     a. Data is proprietary / not publicly available
     b. Code is not available AND architecture details are insufficient
     c. Conflict of interest is severe (company employees validating own product)
     d. No external validation AND no public dataset used
     e. Ground truth labeling methodology is fundamentally flawed

4. 🔍 DEEP PROBE LAYER (MANDATORY — after extraction, before evaluation)
   For EVERY key claim in the paper, generate 3 adversarial questions:
   → WHY this specific design choice? (e.g., "Why 34 layers? Why 12 classes?")
   → WHAT happens without it? (e.g., "What if we use 16 layers? What if we merge similar classes?")
   → WHO benefits from this framing? (e.g., "Does the comparison favor the proposed method?")
   → HOW would a skeptic attack this? (e.g., "Is the comparison fair? Is the baseline weak?")
   → WHERE is the weakest link? (e.g., "Which result would collapse under scrutiny?")
   These questions MUST be answered in the output, even if the answer is "Paper does not address this."

5. EVALUATE using the Adversarial Scientific Checking protocol
   → Apply the domain-specific checklist
   → Classify evidence level (I-VI) per clinical-statistical-framework.md
   → ⚡ ECOSYSTEM BIAS CHECK: Are multiple "validating" papers from the same group/company?
     If yes, downgrade evidence level by 1 and flag: "⚠️ Ecosystem bias: N papers share authors/affiliation/funding"

6. MATCH to the Method Matching Matrix
   → Find the closest entry in the domain methodology file
   → Identify: recommended method, key reference, dataset, difficulty
   → ⚡ REPRODUCIBILITY VERDICT: Can this paper be reproduced with PUBLIC data and PUBLIC code?
     - YES: Provide full reproduction roadmap
     - PARTIAL: Specify what's missing and what can be substituted
     - NO: State clearly "⚠️ This paper CANNOT be fully reproduced with publicly available resources"
       Then provide: "Best-effort reproduction using [alternative dataset] + [architecture from paper]"

7. 🧬 INNOVATION ASSESSMENT (MANDATORY — new)
   → Run the Innovation Assessment Protocol (see below)
   → Score: Innovation Level 1-5
   → Classify: Incremental / Methodological / Conceptual / Paradigm / Disruptive
   → Identify: What is genuinely new vs repackaging vs incremental improvement

8. 🔬 FRONTIER DETECTION (MANDATORY — new)
   → Run the Frontier Detection Protocol (see below)
   → Classify: Established / Emerging / Frontier / Pre-frontier
   → Identify: Research momentum, convergence signals, paradigm shift indicators

9. GENERATE reproduction guidance
   → Use the domain-specific experiment roadmap from physionet-datasets.md
   → Use the reproduction difficulty predictor from the methodology file
   → Use the DL reproduction checklist from deep-learning-bme.md (if applicable)
   → ⚡ PAPER-SPECIFIC OBSTACLES: List obstacles UNIQUE to this paper (not generic roadmap items)
     Example: "Hannun 2019 uses iRhythm Zio data (proprietary) → must substitute with PTB-XL or CinC 2017"

8. OUTPUT using the appropriate module template (Module 1-6 from SKILL.md)
```

### Fatal Blocker Detector

Run this checklist for EVERY paper. If ANY item is 🔴, the paper has a fatal blocker.

| # | Check | 🔴 CRITICAL (Fatal Blocker) | 🟡 WARNING | 🟢 OK |
|---|-------|---------------------------|-----------|-------|
| FB-1 | **Data Availability** | Data is proprietary, no public equivalent exists | Data is proprietary but public substitute exists | Data is public/open-access |
| FB-2 | **Code Availability** | No code, insufficient detail to reimplement | Code available but no pretrained weights, or code is incomplete | Full code + pretrained weights available |
| FB-3 | **Conflict of Interest** | Authors are employees of company whose product is validated; OR study funded by company with equity stake | Authors are consultants/advisors; OR company provided data but no funding | No financial COI related to the product |
| FB-4 | **Ground Truth Quality** | Gold standard labels from same group that developed the model; OR inter-annotator agreement <60%; OR NLP-extracted labels with no manual validation; OR labels from non-experts (students, crowdworkers) without expert review | Labels from independent group but with known biases; OR agreement 60-75%; OR ICD-coded labels; OR non-expert labels with expert review | Labels from independent expert committee; agreement >75% |
| FB-5 | **Comparison Fairness** | Model compared against individual humans but trained on committee consensus; OR CIs overlap but claim "superior"; OR claim scope >> validation scope (e.g., "universal" but tested on 2 paradigms) | Model compared against weak baselines or old methods; CI overlap unclear | Fair comparison with SOTA and clinical baselines; CIs non-overlapping; claim scope = validation scope |
| FB-6 | **External Validation** | No external validation AND no public dataset used; OR single-center dataset with known demographic homogeneity | External validation on different task/dataset from same source; OR multi-center but same demographic region | Independent external validation on different dataset from different institution AND demographic |
| FB-7 | **Reproducibility Path** | Cannot reproduce ANY part of the study | Can reproduce architecture but not data/training; OR can reproduce on substitute data | Can fully reproduce with public resources |
| FB-8 | **Label Noise** | NLP/ICD labels with >30% estimated noise AND no manual validation | NLP/ICD labels with partial manual validation; OR noise rate unknown | Expert-annotated or validated labels; noise rate <15% |
| FB-9 | **Demographic Bias** | Single-center, single-ethnicity data (>80% one group) AND no subgroup analysis; OR model trained on one demographic but deployed on another | Single-center but mixed demographics; OR multi-center same region | Multi-center, multi-demographic with subgroup analysis reported |
| FB-10 | **Causality Claim** | Claims causal relationship from observational/correlational data without causal inference methods | Implies causality but uses hedging language; OR uses "predict" when "associate" is appropriate | Correctly states correlational nature; OR uses proper causal inference methods (IV, DAG, RCT) |
| FB-11 | **Sample Size vs Complexity** | Parameters >> samples (e.g., >10 params per sample); OR no power analysis; OR DNN with <1000 samples and no regularization reported | Moderate overparameterization but with regularization; OR sample size borderline | Adequate sample size for model complexity; power analysis reported |

**Output Format for Fatal Blockers**:
```
⚠️ FATAL BLOCKERS DETECTED
├─ FB-1: 🔴 Data proprietary (iRhythm Zio monitor) — No public equivalent for single-lead 14-day ambulatory ECG
├─ FB-3: 🔴 COI: 2 authors are iRhythm employees, 1 is consultant — This is a product validation study
├─ FB-5: 🟡 Comparison asymmetry: DNN trained on committee labels, compared against individual cardiologists
└─ FB-6: 🟡 External validation only on CinC 2017 (4-class, different label space)

REPRODUCIBILITY VERDICT: ⚠️ CANNOT FULLY REPRODUCE
Best-effort: Use PTB-XL (12-lead) or CinC 2017 (single-lead) as substitute data
```

### Deep Probe Question Templates by Domain

#### ECG Deep Probes
```
1. SIGNAL PROBE
   → Why this lead configuration? What changes with different leads?
   → What sampling rate? Is it sufficient for the target pathology?
   → What preprocessing was applied? Is it the standard pipeline or custom?

2. TASK PROBE
   → Why these specific classes? Are they clinically motivated or data-driven?
   → What about class imbalance? How many samples per class?
   → Is this beat-level, rhythm-level, or record-level classification?

3. DATA PROBE
   → Is the data public? If not, what's the closest public substitute?
   → How was the train/test split done? Patient-wise or record-wise?
   → What's the demographic composition? Age/sex/ethnicity?

4. MODEL PROBE
   → Why this architecture depth/width? Any ablation study?
   → What's the input window length? Why this specific length?
   → How was the model calibrated? Are probability outputs reliable?

5. EVALUATION PROBE
   → Is the comparison against humans fair? (Same data? Same labels? Same constraints?)
   → Are confidence intervals reported? If not, calculate them.
   → What happens at different operating points? Is the ROC curve smooth?

6. CLINICAL PROBE
   → Would this change clinical decisions? At what threshold?
   → What's the regulatory pathway? Has it been submitted?
   → What are the failure modes? What does the model miss?
```

#### EEG/BCI Deep Probes
```
1. SIGNAL PROBE
   → Why this channel count/montage? What's lost with fewer channels?
   → What reference? How does reference choice affect results?
   → What artifact removal? ICA parameters? How many components removed?

2. TASK PROBE
   → Is this within-subject or cross-subject? (Critical for BCI)
   → How many trials per class? Is this sufficient for the model complexity?
   → What's the inter-subject variability in performance?

3. PARADIGM PROBE
   → Is this the standard pipeline for this task? (CSP+LDA for MI, etc.)
   → What does the proposed method add over the standard pipeline?
   → Is the improvement consistent across subjects or driven by outliers?

4. GENERALIZATION PROBE
   → Does it work across sessions? Days? Recording setups?
   → What's the calibration burden for new subjects?
   → Has it been tested on clinical populations (stroke, ALS)?
```

#### Medical Imaging Deep Probes
```
1. DATA PROBE
   → What's the label source? (Expert annotation / NLP extraction / ICD codes)
   → If NLP: What's the estimated noise rate? Has manual validation been done?
   → If expert: How many annotators? What's the inter-annotator agreement?
   → What's the train/test split strategy? Patient-wise or image-wise?
   → Is there data leakage through patient overlap between splits?

2. TASK PROBE
   → Is this multi-label or multi-class? (Chest X-ray is multi-label!)
   → Why these specific pathologies? Are they clinically motivated?
   → What about class imbalance? What's the prevalence of each condition?
   → Is the task clinically meaningful? (Detection vs diagnosis vs triage)

3. COMPARISON PROBE
   → Is the "outperforms radiologists" claim valid?
     → Check: Was the model trained on labels from the SAME annotators used for comparison?
     → Check: Do the 95% CIs overlap? (If yes, claim is NOT statistically supported)
     → Check: How many radiologists? From how many institutions? (Single-institution = low generalizability)
   → Are baselines SOTA? (Compare against CheXpert leaderboard, not just "previous work")
   → Is the comparison metric appropriate? (F1 vs AUROC vs sensitivity at fixed specificity)

4. GENERALIZATION PROBE
   → Has the model been tested on external datasets? (CheXpert → MIMIC-CXR → VinDr-CXR)
   → Does it generalize across demographics? (Age, sex, ethnicity, comorbidities)
   → Does it generalize across imaging protocols? (Portable vs upright, different vendors)
   → What's the domain shift between training and deployment data?
```

#### Clinical Prediction (Tabular) Deep Probes
```
1. LABEL PROBE
   → What's the label source? (ICD codes / clinical diagnosis / lab values)
   → If ICD codes: These are BILLING codes, not clinical diagnoses — what's the noise rate?
   → For mortality: Label is objective and reliable
   → For sepsis: Was Sepsis-3 criteria used? Or ICD-based definition?
   → For phenotype: ICD-based labels have 20-40% noise — was this validated?

2. TIME WINDOW PROBE
   → Why this specific prediction window? (6h / 24h / 48h / full stay)
   → Is the window clinically actionable? (48h may be too late for intervention)
   → What happens with shorter windows? (Trade-off: earlier = more useful but less accurate)
   → Are features available at prediction time? (No temporal leakage)

3. FEATURE PROBE
   → How many features? What's the EPV (events per variable)?
   → How is missing data handled? (Forward fill + indicator? Imputation? Deletion?)
   → Are features clinically meaningful or just "everything available"?
   → Is there feature selection? If so, was it done on the full dataset or only training?

4. EVALUATION PROBE
   → Is AUROC the right metric? (For rare events, AUPRC is more informative)
   → Is calibration assessed? (Overconfident models are dangerous in clinical settings)
   → Was decision curve analysis done? (Clinical utility > statistical performance)
   → Are subgroup results reported? (Performance may vary by age, sex, severity)
```

#### DL-for-BME Deep Probes
```
1. ARCHITECTURE PROBE
   → Why this specific architecture? What's the inductive bias?
   → How many parameters vs training samples? (Risk of overfitting)
   → What's the computational cost? Can it run in real-time?

2. TRAINING PROBE
   → What optimizer? Learning rate schedule? How were these chosen?
   → What regularization? Dropout? Weight decay? Data augmentation?
   → How many epochs? Was early stopping used? On what metric?

3. DATA EFFICIENCY PROBE
   → How much data is actually needed? (Learning curve analysis)
   → What happens with 10x less data? 100x less?
   → Is transfer learning / pretraining used? From what?

4. ROBUSTNESS PROBE
   → How sensitive to hyperparameters? (LR, batch size, architecture width)
   → What happens with noisy inputs? Distribution shift?
   → Is the model well-calibrated? (Reliability diagram)
```

#### Medical Imaging Deep Probes
```
1. IMAGING MODALITY PROBE
   → What modality? (CT, MRI, X-ray, pathology WSI, dermoscopy)
   → Is the method modality-specific or generalizable?
   → How was image quality controlled? (Vendors, protocols, artifacts)

2. SEGMENTATION/DETECTION PROBE
   → What's the annotation protocol? (Expert count, agreement, consensus method)
   → Is the segmentation target well-defined? (Inter-observer variability?)
   → How are edge cases handled? (Ambiguous boundaries, missing structures)

3. SPATIAL RESOLUTION PROBE
   → What's the input resolution? Is it sufficient for the task?
   → How are 3D volumes handled? (2D slices vs 2.5D vs full 3D)
   → Is there multi-scale processing? (Local + global context)

4. CLINICAL WORKFLOW PROBE
   → Where does the AI fit in the clinical workflow? (Triage, diagnosis, quantification)
   → What's the target user? (Radiologist, pathologist, primary care)
   → How would false positives/negatives impact patient care?
```

#### Genomics/Bioinformatics Deep Probes
```
1. DATA QUALITY PROBE
   → What sequencing platform? (Illumina, PacBio, Oxford Nanopore)
   → What's the coverage depth? Is it sufficient for the analysis?
   → How are batch effects handled? (Combat, limma, MNN correction)

2. COMPUTATIONAL PIPELINE PROBE
   → Is the pipeline reproducible? (Snakemake, Nextflow, WDL)
   → What reference genome/annotation version? (GRCh37 vs GRCh38; Ensembl vs RefSeq)
   → Are intermediate QC steps documented? (FastQC, MultiQC, alignment rates)

3. STATISTICAL RIGOR PROBE
   → Multiple testing correction? (Bonferroni, BH-FDR, permutation)
   → Sample size justification? (Power analysis for GWAS, scRNA-seq)
   → Confounding variables controlled? (Population stratification, batch, cell type composition)

4. BIOLOGICAL VALIDATION PROBE
   → Are computational findings experimentally validated?
   → Is there orthogonal evidence? (Multi-omics, literature, functional assays)
   → Are results consistent with known biology? (If not, extraordinary evidence needed)
```

#### Clinical NLP/LLM Deep Probes
```
1. LANGUAGE MODEL PROBE
   → Which base model? What version? What training data?
   → Is the model open-source or API-based? (Reproducibility impact)
   → What's the context window? Is it sufficient for clinical documents?

2. CLINICAL TEXT PROBE
   → What's the text source? (EHR notes, radiology reports, discharge summaries)
   → How was de-identification handled? What's the residual PHI risk?
   → How are abbreviations, negation, and hedging handled?

3. RAG/KNOWLEDGE PROBE
   → What's the retrieval corpus? How current is it?
   → Is retrieval quality measured? (Recall@K, MRR)
   → How are conflicting sources handled? (Guideline updates, contradictory evidence)

4. SAFETY PROBE
   → What's the hallucination rate? How is it measured?
   → Are there guardrails for unsafe medical advice?
   → How does the system handle out-of-scope questions?
   → Is there a human-in-the-loop for clinical decisions?
```

---

## Innovation Assessment Protocol

### Innovation Taxonomy for BME Research

| Level | Type | Definition | Example | Frequency |
|-------|------|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **L1** | Incremental | Apply existing method to new dataset; minor hyperparameter tuning | "ResNet on new ECG dataset" | ~60% of papers |
| **L2** | Methodological | New architecture, loss function, or training strategy within existing paradigm | "Attention mechanism for ECG"; "New data augmentation for EEG" | ~25% of papers |
| **L2b** | Robustness/Reliability | Reveals limitations, fragility, or failure modes of existing methods; establishes reliability bounds | "LLM prompt sensitivity in clinical tasks" (Ceballos 2024); "Failure modes of medical AI" | ~3% of papers |
| **L2e** | AI Safety/Reliability | Develops methods to prevent AI failures in clinical settings: hallucination prevention, bias mitigation, uncertainty quantification | "DrugGPT anti-hallucination for clinical decisions" (Liu 2025); "Conformal prediction for medical AI" | ~2% of papers |
| **L2f** | AI-Driven Experiment Automation | Uses LLM/AI to automate experimental design, protocol generation, or laboratory workflows | "CRISPR-GPT automates gene editing design" (Wang 2025); "AI-driven lab automation" | ~1% of papers |
| **L2c** | Benchmark/Evaluation | Creates new evaluation framework, benchmark suite, or assessment methodology | "MultiMedQA" (Singhal 2023); "HELM for medical LLMs" | ~2% of papers |
| **L2d** | Regulatory/Policy | Develops frameworks for AI governance, approval pathways, or deployment standards | "FDA SaMD framework analysis"; "EU AI Act for medical devices" | ~1% of papers |
| **L3** | Conceptual | New problem formulation; new way to frame an existing task; cross-domain transfer | "Predict EF from ECG" (Attia 2019); "Sleep staging from ear-EEG" | ~10% of papers |
| **L4** | Paradigm | Changes how a field thinks about a problem; enables new categories of research | "End-to-end DL replaces hand-crafted features" (Hannun 2019); "Foundation models for medicine" | ~4% of papers |
| **L5** | Disruptive | Creates entirely new research direction; renders previous approaches obsolete | "Deep learning surpasses human expert diagnosis" (first demonstration in a domain) | ~1% of papers |
| **L5a** | Domain-First Disruptive | First demonstration that AI surpasses human experts in a specific medical domain | Esteva 2017 (dermatology); Hannun 2019 (ECG arrhythmia) | ~1% of papers |
| **L5b** | Historic Problem Solved | Solves a long-standing open problem (>10 years) that many have attempted and failed | AlphaFold2 (50-year protein folding problem); CRISPR gene editing | <1% of papers |
| **L5c** | Global Disruptive | Creates paradigm shift across multiple disciplines, not just one domain | Transformer architecture; Deep learning itself; Internet | <0.1% of papers |

**Note**: L2b/L2c/L2d/L2e/L2f are subtypes of L2 that have distinct evaluation criteria. They score as L2 on the Innovation Level but receive special handling in assessment.

### AI-Driven Scientific Discovery Assessment

A new paradigm is emerging where AI generates testable hypotheses that are then experimentally validated:

| Discovery Type | Definition | Example | Innovation Level |
|---------------|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| **Hypothesis Generation** | AI proposes new hypotheses from data patterns | C2S-Scale 27B identifies drug combinations for immune activation | L3 (if experimentally validated) |
| **Experiment Design** | AI designs optimal experiments to test hypotheses | CRISPR-GPT designs gene editing protocols | L2f |
| **Autonomous Discovery** | AI designs, executes, and interprets experiments without human intervention | Self-driving labs for drug screening | L4 (if fully autonomous) |
| **Mechanism Discovery** | AI uncovers previously unknown biological mechanisms | AlphaFold reveals protein dynamics | L4-L5 |

**Rule**: AI-generated hypotheses WITHOUT experimental validation should be scored as L2 at most. Hypotheses WITH independent experimental validation can be scored as L3+.
**Rule**: When evaluating AI-driven discovery papers, assess: (1) Was the discovery made by AI or by humans using AI as a tool? (2) Was the discovery experimentally validated? (3) Could the discovery have been made without AI?

### Clinical Validation Level Classification

| Level | Description | Evidence Strength | Example |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|---------|
| **V0** | No validation (theory only) | None | Theoretical proposals without any data |
| **V1** | Internal validation only | Weak — may overfit | Train/test split on single dataset |
| **V2** | External validation (different center/data) | Moderate — shows generalizability | Multi-center retrospective study |
| **V3** | Prospective silent trial | Strong — real-world data, no intervention | EAGLE (2025): prospective blinded trial on clinical samples |
| **V4** | Prospective interventional study | Very strong — AI influences clinical decisions | AI-assisted diagnosis with clinician in the loop |
| **V5** | Randomized controlled trial | Gold standard — controls for confounders | RCT comparing AI-assisted vs standard care |

**Rule**: Papers at V0-V1 should have their clinical claims flagged: "⚠️ Limited validation — claims require external confirmation."
**Rule**: Papers at V3+ represent meaningful clinical translation. V3 is significantly stronger than V2 — do not treat them as equivalent.

### Non-AI Biomedical Innovation Assessment

This framework primarily focuses on AI in BME, but important non-AI innovations also occur:

| Innovation Type | Description | How to Assess | Example |
|----------------|-------------|---------------|---------|
| **Novel Biomaterial** | New material for medical devices or tissue engineering | Assess: biocompatibility, functional performance, novelty of material properties | 3D-printed tissue scaffolds |
| **Novel Diagnostic Method** | New non-AI diagnostic technology | Assess: sensitivity, specificity, cost, accessibility, regulatory pathway | LUCAS bioluminescence detection (2025) |
| **Novel Drug Delivery** | New delivery mechanism or formulation | Assess: targeting efficiency, safety, pharmacokinetics, clinical benefit | Albumin-hitchhiking nanobody (2025) |
| **Novel Surgical Technique** | New surgical approach or device | Assess: patient outcomes, learning curve, complication rates, scalability | Minimally invasive robotic surgery |
| **Novel Biosensor** | New sensing principle or device | Assess: detection limit, specificity, stability, point-of-care suitability | Wearable continuous lactate monitor |

**Rule**: When evaluating non-AI innovations, use the same Innovation Taxonomy (L1-L5) but replace AI-specific dimensions with domain-specific ones. Focus on: (1) Clinical impact, (2) Technical novelty, (3) Scalability, (4) Regulatory feasibility.

### Industry Release vs Academic Publication

| Type | Description | Evidence Level | How to Handle |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Peer-reviewed publication** | Published in journal with peer review | Standard academic evidence | Normal assessment |
| **Preprint** | Posted on arXiv/bioRxiv without peer review | Lower — may contain errors | Flag: "Preprint — not yet peer-reviewed. Verify claims independently." |
| **Industry announcement** | Press release, blog post, or conference demo | Lowest — no independent verification | Flag: "Industry announcement — no peer review. Claims should be treated as marketing until independently verified." |
| **Regulatory submission** | FDA/EMA filing with clinical data | High — regulatory review | Assess regulatory data; note that full data may not be public |

**Rule**: Industry announcements (e.g., "盘古医疗3.0", "GPT-5 Medical") without peer-reviewed publications should be flagged and scored conservatively. Innovation claims from industry releases should be verified against independent evidence.

### Bias/Fairness Analysis Severity Scale

When evaluating L2b papers that address bias or fairness, classify the severity:

| Severity | Definition | Impact | Example | Framework Action |
|----------|-----------|--------|---------|-----------------|
| **S1: Lab Discovery** | Bias found in research model, not deployed | Academic concern; informs future development | Seyyed-Kalantari 2021 (CX underdiagnosis in public datasets) | Standard L2b scoring |
| **S2: Pre-deployment Warning** | Bias found in model being considered for deployment | May prevent harmful deployment | Glocker 2023 (CX models encode race from non-clinical features) | Add flag: "⚠️ Pre-deployment bias — must be addressed before clinical use" |
| **S3: Deployed with Known Bias** | Algorithm deployed despite known bias concerns | Active patient harm; health inequity | Beede 2020 (dermatology AI worse on dark skin, deployed in limited settings) | Add flag: "🔴 Known bias in deployed system — active patient harm risk" |
| **S4: Widespread Deployment Failure** | Algorithm deployed at scale with significant failure/bias | Large-scale patient harm; alert fatigue; loss of trust | Epic Sepsis Model (low sensitivity, high false positive rate at hundreds of hospitals) | Add flag: "🔴 Widespread deployment failure — this case should be studied as a negative example" |

**Bias Subtype Classification**:
| Subtype | Description | Detection |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| **Label Bias** | Training labels reflect systemic disparities (e.g., cost as proxy for need) | Check: Does the label directly measure the outcome of interest, or a proxy? |
| **Representation Bias** | Underrepresented groups in training data | Check: Is training data demographically diverse? Are subgroup results reported? |
| **Feature Leakage/Shortcut Learning** | Model uses non-clinical features (scanner markers, text patterns) as shortcuts | Check: Does the model perform well on out-of-distribution data? Can it be fooled by spurious features? |
| **Measurement Bias** | Different quality of input data across groups | Check: Is sensor/image quality consistent across demographic groups? |
| **Evaluation Bias** | Metrics that mask subgroup disparities | Check: Are AUROC/AUPRC reported per subgroup? Is overall metric hiding poor subgroup performance? |
| **Deployment Bias** | Model behavior changes in real-world use vs lab evaluation | Check: Is there post-deployment monitoring? Does real-world performance match lab results? |

**Rule**: If a paper reveals S3+ severity bias, its Impact Potential score should be elevated (minimum 6/10) regardless of methodological novelty — the impact of preventing patient harm is significant.
**Rule**: If a paper reveals S4 severity (widespread deployment failure), classify as "Negative Landmark" — not innovative in method, but critically important for the field.

### Clinical Deployment Failure Analysis Framework

When analyzing an AI system that failed in clinical deployment:

| Dimension | Questions to Ask | Red Flags |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Deployment Scale** | How many sites? How many patients affected? | >10 sites OR >10,000 patients = large-scale failure |
| **Failure Mode** | What specifically failed? (accuracy, workflow, alert fatigue, bias) | Multiple failure modes = systemic problem |
| **Root Cause** | Was it a technical, clinical, or implementation failure? | Technical: model doesn't work; Clinical: wrong problem; Implementation: doesn't fit workflow |
| **Detection Method** | How was the failure discovered? (internal monitoring, external audit, patient harm) | Discovered only after patient harm = monitoring failure |
| **Remediation** | Was the system fixed, withdrawn, or still in use? | Still in use despite known failure = institutional failure |
| **Lessons** | What can the field learn from this failure? | No lessons published = missed learning opportunity |

**Rule**: Every clinical deployment failure should be documented as a case study with: System Name, Scale, Failure Mode, Root Cause, Detection Method, Remediation Status, Key Lessons.

### Cross-Domain Impact Papers

Some papers published outside BME have major implications for BME research:

| Paper | Original Domain | BME Impact | How to Handle |
|-------|----------------|------------|---------------|
| Obermeyer 2019 (racial bias in health algorithm) | Health policy/ML | Directly relevant to all clinical AI | Include in BME analysis with note: "Originally published in [domain], directly applicable to BME" |
| Buolamwini 2018 (Gender Shades) | Fairness/AI | Relevant to medical imaging bias | Include with note: "General AI fairness finding with BME implications" |
| Vaswani 2017 (Transformer) | NLP | Foundation of all medical transformers | Include with note: "Foundational architecture adopted by BME" |
| Devlin 2019 (BERT) | NLP | Foundation of ClinicalBERT and medical LLMs | Include with note: "Pre-training paradigm adapted for clinical text" |
| Ho 2020 (Denoising Diffusion) | Computer vision | Medical image synthesis and anomaly detection | Include with note: "Generative model adapted for medical imaging" |

**Rule**: When a non-BME paper is cited in BME research, identify: (1) Original contribution, (2) How it was adapted for BME, (3) What was lost/gained in adaptation.

### Innovation Scoring Rubric (0-10)

| Dimension | 0-2 (Low) | 3-5 (Medium) | 6-8 (High) | 9-10 (Exceptional) |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|-------------------|
| **Novelty** | Reproduces known result | New combination of known methods | New method or new problem formulation | Creates new paradigm or research direction |
| **Impact Potential** | Marginal improvement | Meaningful improvement on established benchmark | Opens new application or significantly advances SOTA | Enables previously impossible applications |
| **Generalizability** | Task-specific, no transfer | Transferable within domain | Transferable across domains | Universal principle or framework |
| **Rigor** | Weak validation | Standard validation | Extensive validation + ablation | Comprehensive: multi-center, multi-task, theoretical grounding |
| **Timeliness** | Behind current trends | Aligned with current trends | Anticipates next trend | Defines the next trend |
| **Cross-Domain Fusion** | Single domain, no fusion | Combines methods from 2 domains (e.g., vision+language, signal+imaging) | Novel fusion creating new capabilities | Fusion enables previously impossible cross-domain tasks |
| **Efficiency/Accessibility** | No efficiency gain; same or more resources | Marginal efficiency gain (2-5× reduction in compute/data/params) | Significant efficiency gain (5-50×); enables wider adoption | Order-of-magnitude gain (>50×); democratizes previously exclusive capability |

**Innovation Score = Sum of 7 dimensions / 70 × 10**
**Rating**: 0-3=Incremental; 4-5=Noteworthy; 6-7=Significant; 8-9=Landmark; 10=Potential Breakthrough

### Generalist vs Specialist Trade-off Assessment

For papers proposing generalist/universal models (e.g., Med-PaLM M, BiomedCLIP, SAM):

| Dimension | Specialist Model | Generalist Model | Assessment Rule |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Single-task Accuracy** | Should be SOTA or near-SOTA | May be below specialist SOTA | Generalist model below specialist SOTA is NOT a flaw — it's expected |
| **Cross-task Transfer** | N/A | Key metric — zero-shot/few-shot on unseen tasks | If generalist cannot transfer → major flaw |
| **Efficiency per Task** | Optimized for one task | Amortized cost across tasks | Generalist is more efficient if it serves N tasks with <N× compute |
| **Deployment Flexibility** | Single-purpose | Multi-purpose | Generalist value = breadth × acceptable accuracy |

**Rule**: When evaluating a generalist model, do NOT penalize for being below specialist SOTA on individual tasks. Instead, evaluate: (1) How many tasks does it cover? (2) Is accuracy on each task clinically acceptable? (3) Does zero-shot/few-shot transfer work?
**Rule**: If a generalist model claims to surpass specialist models on ALL tasks, flag: "⚠️ Extraordinary claim — verify that specialist baselines are properly tuned and evaluated on the same data splits."

### Foundation Model Tier Classification

| Tier | Definition | Example | Innovation Implication |
|------|-----------|---------|----------------------|
| **T1: General Foundation** | Trained on general data, not domain-specific | GPT-4, CLIP, SAM | Innovation = adaptation method, not the model itself |
| **T2: Domain Foundation** | Pretrained on domain-specific data at scale | BiomedCLIP, RadImageNet, UNI | Innovation = data curation + pretraining strategy |
| **T3: Task-Specific Foundation** | Pretrained for a specific task family | CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR models | Innovation = task formulation + architecture |
| **T4: Multi-modal Foundation** | Integrates multiple modalities in pretraining | Med-PaLM M, BiomedParse | Innovation = fusion architecture + multi-task learning |

**Rule**: When a paper uses a T1 model (e.g., GPT-4) and fine-tunes for medical tasks, the innovation belongs to the fine-tuning/adaptation method, NOT the base model. Score accordingly.
**Rule**: When a paper creates a T2+ foundation model, evaluate: (1) Scale of pretraining data, (2) Diversity of pretraining tasks, (3) Downstream task coverage, (4) Data scale innovation (million→billion→trillion).

### Data Scale Innovation Assessment

| Scale | Description | Innovation Implication |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|
| **S1: Standard** | <100K samples | No scale innovation |
| **S2: Large** | 100K-1M samples | Moderate scale; requires engineering |
| **S3: Very Large** | 1M-100M samples | Significant scale; requires distributed training |
| **S4: Massive** | 100M-1B samples | Major scale innovation; new data curation challenges |
| **S5: Unprecedented** | >1B samples | Historic scale; enables emergent capabilities |

**Rule**: Data scale innovation alone is NOT sufficient for L3+ innovation. It must be accompanied by: (1) Novel data curation methodology, (2) Demonstrated emergent capabilities at scale, OR (3) New training paradigm enabled by scale.

**Cross-Domain Fusion Subtypes** (for detailed analysis):
| Fusion Type | Example | Key Challenge |
|------------|---------|--------------|
| **Vision + Language** | PathChat, LLaVA-Med, BiomedCLIP (pretrain alignment) | Aligning visual and textual representations; grounding language in image regions |
| **Signal + Imaging** | ECG + CXR multimodal, EEG + fMRI | Temporal-spatial alignment; different sampling rates and resolutions |
| **Genomic + Clinical** | Multi-omics + EHR, drug response prediction | Heterogeneous data types; missing data; different scales |
| **Genomic + Imaging** | Radiomics + WGS, imaging biomarkers + genomics | Feature dimensionality mismatch; different sample sizes per modality |
| **Signal + Signal** | ECG + PPG, EEG + EMG | Sensor fusion; cross-modal noise; channel alignment |
| **Text + Structured** | Clinical notes + lab values | Free text to structured mapping; temporal alignment |
| **Text + Imaging** | Radiology report + imaging, pathology report + WSI | Report grounding in image regions; cross-attention alignment |
| **Multi-modal (3+)** | ICU: notes + labs + signals; cancer: imaging + genomics + clinical | Fusion architecture complexity; missing modality handling; modality weighting |

**Fusion Stage Classification**:
| Stage | Description | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| **Pretrain Alignment** | Align representations during pretraining (e.g., contrastive learning) | BiomedCLIP, ConVIRT |
| **Feature Fusion** | Combine extracted features at intermediate layer | Late fusion, attention-based fusion |
| **Decision Fusion** | Combine predictions from separate models per modality | Ensemble of unimodal predictors |
| **End-to-End Fusion** | Joint training from raw inputs to final prediction | Multimodal transformer |

### Modality Compatibility Check (Run BEFORE evaluating Cross-Domain Fusion)

| Compatibility Issue | Description | Check | Example |
|--------------------|-------------|-------|---------|
| **Temporal Resolution Mismatch** | Different sampling rates between modalities | EEG (ms) + fMRI (s): 1000× resolution gap → requires careful downsampling or interpolation | EEG+fMRI fusion: must address temporal mismatch explicitly |
| **Spatial Resolution Mismatch** | Different spatial granularity | Genomics (molecular) + Imaging (mm): fundamentally different spatial scales | Genomic+Imaging: requires intermediate representation |
| **Sample Size Asymmetry** | Different number of samples per modality | Genomics (n=100) + Clinical (n=10000): missing data for most samples | Multi-omics: must handle missing modalities |
| **Temporal Alignment** | Events in different modalities may not be temporally aligned | ECG event (real-time) vs clinical note (hours later) | Signal+Text: must establish temporal correspondence |
| **Semantic Gap** | Different modalities encode fundamentally different information | ECG (electrical) + CXR (structural): related but not directly comparable | Signal+Imaging: requires domain-specific bridging |

**Rule**: If a paper claims multi-modal fusion but does NOT address the relevant compatibility issues from the table above, flag: "⚠️ Modality compatibility not addressed — [specify which issues]. This may undermine fusion effectiveness."
**Rule**: If temporal resolution mismatch is >100× and the paper does not explicitly address it, flag: "🔴 Severe temporal resolution mismatch — fusion may be unreliable without careful alignment."

### Fusion Benefit-Cost Ratio Assessment

Multi-modal fusion adds complexity — is the benefit worth the cost?

| Fusion Benefit | Measurement | Threshold for Worthwhile Fusion |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **AUC Improvement** | ΔAUC = AUC_multimodal - AUC_best_unimodal | ΔAUC > 0.02 (2 percentage points) = worthwhile; ΔAUC < 0.01 = questionable value |
| **Clinical Metric Improvement** | ΔSensitivity/Specificity at fixed threshold | Must be clinically meaningful (e.g., >5% sensitivity improvement for screening) |
| **New Capability** | Tasks impossible with single modality | Any new capability = worthwhile (e.g., cross-modal prediction) |
| **Robustness Improvement** | Performance on OOD data, missing modality | Significant robustness gain = worthwhile even without AUC improvement |

**Fusion Cost Assessment**:
| Cost Factor | Description | When to Flag |
|------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Data Collection** | Additional modality requires extra data collection | If additional modality is expensive/rare (e.g., genomic + imaging) |
| **Computational** | Training and inference cost increases | If compute increases >5× for <2% AUC gain |
| **Missing Data** | Not all samples have all modalities | If >30% missing for any modality → fusion may be unreliable |
| **Maintenance** | Multi-modal pipeline harder to maintain | If deployment requires multiple data streams in real-time |

**Rule**: If a paper claims multi-modal fusion but ΔAUC < 0.01 over best unimodal baseline, flag: "⚠️ Marginal fusion benefit — additional complexity may not be justified by <1% AUC improvement."
**Rule**: If a paper does NOT compare against strong unimodal baselines, flag: "🔴 No unimodal comparison — fusion benefit cannot be assessed."

### LLM Visual Interpretation Shortcut Detection

When evaluating papers that use LLMs (GPT-4V, GPT-4o, etc.) to interpret medical images:

| Shortcut Type | Description | Detection Method |
|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Text in Image** | LLM reads text/labels on the image rather than analyzing visual features | Remove text from image (redact labels, patient info, device readings) and re-evaluate |
| **Metadata Leakage** | LLM uses EXIF data, image format, or file metadata | Strip metadata and re-evaluate |
| **Template Recognition** | LLM recognizes image template/layout rather than content | Use images from different devices/formats |
| **Prompt Leakage** | Prompt contains information that gives away the answer | Use neutral prompts without diagnostic hints |

**Rule**: If a paper uses LLM for medical image interpretation but does NOT control for text-in-image shortcuts, flag: "⚠️ LLM visual shortcut not excluded — model may be reading text labels rather than analyzing visual features. Verify with text-redacted images."
**Rule**: If a paper reports LLM accuracy on ECG images but the ECG paper contains diagnostic text, flag: "🔴 Potential text shortcut — ECG images often contain computer-generated diagnostic text that LLMs can read directly."

### Validation Sufficiency Auto-Check

Automatically check validation sufficiency when analyzing any medical AI paper:

| Check | Question | Flag if Failed |
|-------|----------|---------------|
| **External Validation** | Was the model tested on data from a different center/institution? | 🟡 "Internal validation only — generalizability unknown" (applies to 96.3% of papers) |
| **Temporal Validation** | Was the model tested on data from a different time period? | 🟡 "No temporal validation — model may not generalize to future data" |
| **Prospective Validation** | Was the model tested prospectively (not retrospectively)? | 🟡 "Retrospective only — clinical performance may differ" |
| **Uncertainty Quantification** | Does the model report confidence/uncertainty in predictions? | 🟡 "No uncertainty quantification — model cannot detect when it may be wrong" |
| **Subgroup Analysis** | Are results reported per demographic/clinical subgroup? | 🟡 "No subgroup analysis — performance may vary across populations" |

**Rule**: If a paper has ONLY internal validation (V1) AND makes clinical claims, flag: "🔴 Clinical claims with internal validation only — 96.3% of medical AI papers lack external validation. Independent replication required before clinical trust."

### LLM Research Reproducibility Checklist

When evaluating papers that use LLMs (GPT-4, Claude, etc.) for medical tasks:

| Item | Required? | Why It Matters |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| **LLM name and version** | ✅ Mandatory | GPT-3.5 ≠ GPT-4 ≠ GPT-4o; results not comparable |
| **Date of LLM access** | ✅ Mandatory | LLMs are updated silently; results may change over time |
| **Prompt template** | ✅ Mandatory | Small prompt changes can cause large result variations |
| **Temperature and parameters** | ✅ Mandatory | Temperature=0 vs Temperature=1 gives very different results |
| **Number of runs** | ✅ Mandatory | LLM outputs are stochastic; single run is unreliable |
| **Human annotation protocol** | ✅ If used | Annotator expertise and inter-rater agreement must be reported |
| **API vs local model** | ✅ Mandatory | API results may change without notice; local models are reproducible |

**Rule**: If a paper uses an LLM but does NOT report version, date, and prompt, flag: "🔴 LLM reproducibility failure — missing [version/date/prompt]. Results cannot be independently verified."
**Rule**: If a paper uses an LLM with API access (not local), flag: "⚠️ API-based LLM — results may change without notice. No version control possible."

### Innovation Red Flags — "Fake Innovation" Detection

| Red Flag | Description | Detection Method |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Frankenstein Model** | Combining 3+ existing modules and claiming novelty | Check: Is the combination non-obvious? Does it outperform simpler combinations? |
| **Dataset Tourism** | Applying same method to new dataset without adaptation | Check: Is any methodological change made for the new dataset? Or just re-training? |
| **Metric Gaming** | Novel metric that makes the proposed method look best | Check: Does the method win on standard metrics? If only on custom metric → suspicious |
| **Straw Baseline** | Comparing against intentionally weak baselines | Check: Are baselines from the same year? Are they properly tuned? |
| **Orphan Innovation** | Novel component that doesn't actually improve performance | Check: Ablation study — does removing the "novel" component hurt performance? |
| **Hype Inflation** | Using trendy terms (transformer, foundation model, digital twin) for incremental work | Check: Does the method actually require/use the claimed technology? |

**Rule**: If a paper has ≥2 red flags, downgrade Innovation Level by 1 and flag: "⚠️ Innovation inflation detected — [specify which red flags]."

### Scope Expansion Innovation Assessment

When a paper extends an existing method from one domain to a broader scope:

| Scope Expansion Type | Definition | Innovation Level | Example |
|---------------------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| **Horizontal Expansion** | Same method applied to new but related domain | L1-L2 (incremental) | ECG model applied to PPG |
| **Vertical Expansion** | Method extended to handle more complex inputs within same domain | L2-L3 (significant) | Protein structure → Protein+ligand complex |
| **Paradigm Expansion** | Method fundamentally reworked to handle qualitatively different entities | L3-L4 (major) | AlphaFold2 (proteins) → AlphaFold3 (all biomolecules) |
| **Universe Expansion** | Method becomes a general-purpose framework applicable across disciplines | L4-L5 (landmark) | Transformer (NLP) → Vision Transformer → Universal |

**Rule**: If a paper claims "generalization" but only adds new data types without architectural change, score as L2 max. True scope expansion requires methodological innovation to handle the new domain.
**Rule**: AlphaFold3 (2024) = Paradigm Expansion (L4): same core idea (Evoformer) but fundamentally reworked (diffusion module, Pairformer) to handle all biomolecule types. NOT just "AlphaFold2 with more data."

### Generative vs Predictive Innovation Distinction

A critical distinction that the framework must make:

| Innovation Paradigm | What It Does | Innovation Implication | Examples |
|--------------------|-------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Predictive** | Predicts properties of EXISTING entities | Standard innovation assessment | AlphaFold2 predicts protein structure; DL predicts disease from ECG |
| **Generative** | Creates NEW entities that did not exist before | Higher innovation ceiling — creates rather than analyzes | RFdiffusion designs new proteins; generative chemistry designs new molecules |
| **Optimizing** | Finds optimal configurations within defined space | Moderate innovation — extends search capability | Bayesian optimization for experiment design; hyperparameter optimization |
| **Autonomous** | Independently designs, executes, and interprets experiments | Highest innovation ceiling — replaces human in the loop | AI-Scientist; self-driving labs |

**Rule**: Generative innovation should be scored 1 level higher than equivalent predictive innovation, because creating new entities is fundamentally harder than analyzing existing ones.
**Rule**: If a paper generates new molecular/protein designs that are experimentally validated, score as L3+ minimum. Unvalidated generative work = L2 max.
**Rule**: RFdiffusion (2023) = L4 (Generative, experimentally validated); RFdiffusion2 (2025) = L4-L5 (Generative with active site control, expanding scope); AlphaFold2 = L5b (Predictive, historic problem solved); AlphaFold3 = L4 (Predictive+Generative hybrid, scope expansion)

### AI Drug Discovery Pipeline Innovation Assessment

AI drug discovery involves a multi-stage pipeline. Innovation assessment must consider which stages AI contributed to:

| Pipeline Stage | AI Contribution | Innovation Level | Example |
|---------------|----------------|-----------------|---------|
| **Target Discovery** | AI identifies novel drug target | L3 (if validated) | Insilico Pharma.AI identified TNIK as target |
| **Molecular Design** | AI designs novel molecule for target | L3 (if synthesized) | Insilico generated INS018_055 structure |
| **Property Prediction** | AI predicts ADMET properties | L2 (incremental) | Standard QSAR/QSPR models |
| **Clinical Trial Design** | AI optimizes trial protocol | L2-L3 | Adaptive trial design with AI |
| **Full Pipeline** | AI contributes to ALL stages from target to clinic | L4-L5 (landmark) | Insilico Rentosertib: AI target discovery + AI molecular design + Phase IIa validation |

**Rule**: If a paper claims "AI-discovered drug" but AI only contributed to one stage (e.g., only molecular design, target was known), score as L3 max. Full pipeline = L4+.
**Rule**: Rentosertib/INS018_055 (Insilico, Nature Medicine 2025) = L4-L5: First AI-discovered AND AI-designed drug to reach Phase IIa with positive results. This is the strongest clinical validation of AI-driven drug discovery to date.
**Rule**: When evaluating AI drug discovery papers, ALWAYS check: (1) Which pipeline stages did AI contribute to? (2) Is the target novel or known? (3) Has the drug been tested in humans? (4) Is the AI contribution independently verifiable?

### AI Autonomy Level Classification

| Level | Name | Description | Innovation Implication | Example |
|-------|------|-------------|----------------------|---------|
| **A0** | AI as Tool | Human uses AI output directly; no autonomous decision-making | Standard innovation assessment | Using GPT-4 to summarize papers |
| **A1** | AI-Assisted | AI suggests options; human makes final decision | +0 to innovation score | AI suggests drug candidates; chemist selects |
| **A2** | AI-Guided | AI designs experiments; human executes and interprets | +1 to innovation score | CRISPR-GPT designs gene editing protocol |
| **A3** | AI-Supervised Autonomous | AI designs and executes; human supervises and approves | +1-2 to innovation score | Self-driving lab with human checkpoint |
| **A4** | Fully Autonomous | AI designs, executes, interprets, and iterates without human intervention | +2 to innovation score (if validated) | AI-Scientist (Sakana 2025) |

**Rule**: Papers claiming A3+ autonomy must provide evidence of: (1) Number of autonomous decisions made, (2) Human intervention rate, (3) Success rate of autonomous decisions vs human-guided decisions.
**Rule**: A4 claims without experimental validation should be scored as A2 max (provisional).

### System-Level Innovation Assessment

When a paper presents a complete system (hardware + software + clinical workflow):

| Component | Innovation Assessment | Example |
|-----------|----------------------|---------|
| **Hardware Innovation** | Novel sensor, device, or form factor | Flexible ECG patch with optimal wear positioning |
| **Software/Algorithm Innovation** | Novel AI model or signal processing | IAT model for ECG diagnosis; GAN for wear correction |
| **Workflow Innovation** | Novel clinical integration or deployment strategy | Real-time diagnosis + GPT-based medical consultation |
| **Integration Innovation** | Novel combination of existing components into a system | Combining wearable + AI + clinical workflow |

**Rule**: System-level papers should be scored on EACH component separately, then given an Integration Bonus of +1 if the whole is greater than the sum of parts (demonstrated by ablation showing system > best component alone).
**Rule**: If a system paper uses only existing components without ANY novel component, score as L1-L2 (integration only). Novel integration = L2-L3; Novel component + novel integration = L3+.

### Nobel/Major Award Validation Mechanism

| Award Type | Signal Strength | How to Handle |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Nobel Prize** | Strongest possible independent validation | Automatically upgrade Frontier Stage to "Established-Classic"; Innovation Level is confirmed (not upgraded — the level was already correct) |
| **Lasker Award** | Very strong — often predicts Nobel | Confirm Innovation Level; add "Lasker Award validated" |
| **Breakthrough Prize** | Strong — recognizes paradigm-shifting work | Confirm Innovation Level; add "Breakthrough Prize validated" |
| **Best Paper (top venue)** | Moderate — venue-specific recognition | Note but do not change Innovation Level |
| **Highly Cited (>5000)** | Moderate — community adoption | Add Influence Bonus (+1) if not already applied |

**Rule**: Nobel Prize = confirmation of L4+ innovation, NOT an upgrade. If a paper won a Nobel but was previously scored L3, re-examine the scoring — the Nobel confirms the work was L4+ all along.
**Rule**: AlphaFold2 winning 2024 Nobel Chemistry = confirms L5b/c classification. The Nobel does not change the score; it validates it.
