| Level | Name | Requirement | Clinical Implication |
|-------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **BCI-V0** | Lab Acute | Performance in controlled lab, single session | Research only |
| **BCI-V1** | Lab Longitudinal | Stable performance in lab over ≥6 months | Promising; home use not validated |
| **BCI-V2** | Supervised Home | Home use with technical supervision | Feasibility demonstrated |
| **BCI-V3** | Independent Home | Independent home use with remote monitoring | Clinical utility demonstrated |
| **BCI-V4** | Standard of Care | BCI as standard assistive technology | Widely deployable |

**Rule**: Neuralink PRIME (2024) achieved BCI-V1 (lab longitudinal) with human cursor control. Home use (BCI-V2+) is still pending. Do NOT overclaim based on lab-only results.
**Rule**: BCI papers that report only "peak performance" without longitudinal tracking are INCOMPLETE. The clinically relevant metric is SUSTAINED performance, not peak performance.
**Rule**: The lab-to-home performance gap for BCI is typically 15-30% degradation. Any paper claiming comparable lab and home performance MUST provide detailed evidence of environmental control.

### Spatial Biology + AI Assessment Protocol

When AI is applied to spatial transcriptomics or spatial proteomics data:

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Spatial Resolution Adequacy** | Is the spatial resolution sufficient to answer the biological question? | Resolution vs feature size comparison; spot/cell-level validation | Single-cell resolution claimed from spot-level data |
| **Technology Bias Correction** | Has AI corrected for technology-specific artifacts? | Cross-platform validation; technology-specific bias characterization | No cross-platform validation; AI trained on single platform |
| **Spatial Pattern Reproducibility** | Are identified spatial patterns reproducible? | Cross-sample validation; spatial pattern stability across batches | Patterns found in single sample only |
| **Biological Plausibility** | Do AI-identified spatial patterns match known tissue architecture? | Comparison with histopathology; known tissue structure validation | AI finds patterns contradicting established tissue biology |
| **Multi-Modal Integration** | Does AI integrate spatial + non-spatial data appropriately? | Integration method validation; comparison with single-modality baselines | Spatial information not utilized; AI ignores spatial structure |
| **Clinical Correlation** | Do spatial patterns correlate with clinical outcomes? | Survival analysis; treatment response correlation; independent validation cohort | Only molecular-level validation; no clinical outcome correlation |

**Spatial Biology AI Validation Levels**:

| Level | Name | Requirement | Clinical Implication |
|-------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **SB-V0** | In Silico | AI applied to simulated/public spatial data | Research tool only |
| **SB-V1** | Single-Platform | Validated on one spatial transcriptomics platform | Platform-specific |
| **SB-V2** | Cross-Platform | Validated across multiple spatial platforms | Robust to technology |
| **SB-V3** | Clinical Correlation | Spatial patterns correlated with clinical outcomes | Clinically relevant |
| **SB-V4** | Clinical Utility | Spatial AI guides clinical decisions with outcome improvement | Clinical impact demonstrated |

**Rule**: Spatial transcriptomics AI that does not validate against histopathology is INCOMPLETE. The ground truth for tissue architecture is pathology, not another AI model.
**Rule**: "AI discovers new cell type" in spatial data MUST be validated by: (1) marker gene expression, (2) spatial coherence, (3) cross-platform reproducibility, (4) functional validation. Without all four, it is an UNVERIFIED COMPUTATIONAL ARTIFACT.
**Rule**: MERFISH, Visium HD, and CODEX have different resolution and bias profiles. AI trained on one platform may not generalize. Always assess cross-platform robustness.

### AI for Surgery Assessment Protocol

When AI is applied to surgical planning, intraoperative guidance, or autonomous surgery:

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Surgical Phase Recognition** | Can AI accurately identify surgical phases in real-time? | Frame-level accuracy; temporal consistency; cross-surgeon validation | Only offline analysis; no real-time demonstration |
| **Anatomical Localization** | Can AI accurately localize anatomical structures during surgery? | Localization accuracy vs surgeon annotation; margin assessment; deformation handling | Only pre-operative imaging; no intraoperative validation |
| **Risk Prediction** | Can AI predict intraoperative complications? | Prospective complication prediction; lead time for intervention; false alarm rate | Only retrospective complication association |
| **Autonomy Level** | What level of surgical autonomy does the system provide? | Clear autonomy classification; human override mechanism; failure mode analysis | Ambiguous autonomy claims; no override mechanism |
| **Surgeon Performance Impact** | Does AI improve surgeon performance? | RCT: surgeon+AI vs surgeon alone; operative time, complication rate, learning curve | Only AI accuracy reported; no surgeon performance impact |
| **Safety Margin** | What safety mechanisms prevent AI-caused surgical errors? | Safety constraints; hard stops; human-in-the-loop verification; failure rate analysis | No safety constraints; AI can execute without verification |

**Surgical AI Autonomy Levels**:

| Level | Name | AI Role | Human Role | Validation Requirement |
|-------|------|---------|-----------|----------------------|
| **SA-L0** | No Autonomy | Information display only | Full surgical control | Display accuracy validation |
| **SA-L1** | Surgical Assistance | Robot executes surgeon's commands | Direct control; AI assists | Execution accuracy |
| **SA-L2** | Partial Autonomy | AI performs specific subtasks under supervision | Supervises; can override | Subtask safety + override reliability |
| **SA-L3** | Conditional Autonomy | AI performs defined surgical tasks autonomously within constraints | Monitors; intervenes for exceptions | Task-level safety + exception handling |
| **SA-L4** | High Autonomy | AI performs entire surgical procedure autonomously | Pre-operative planning; post-operative review | Full procedure safety + regulatory approval |

**Rule**: Surgical AI at SA-L2 and above MUST have a human override mechanism that can halt AI action within 1 second. No override = no clinical deployment.
**Rule**: AI surgical planning without intraoperative adaptation is INCOMPLETE. Tissue deformation and anatomical variation mean pre-operative plans must be updated in real-time.
**Rule**: The Intuitive Surgical da Vinci system is SA-L1 (surgical assistance). No surgical AI system has achieved SA-L3+ in clinical practice. Any claim of "autonomous surgery" must be scrutinized.

### Liquid Biopsy AI Assessment Protocol

When AI is applied to liquid biopsy data (ctDNA, CTC, exosomes, cfRNA):

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Analytical Sensitivity** | What is the minimum variant allele frequency (VAF) the AI can detect? | Limit of detection (LOD) at known VAF; serial dilution validation | LOD not established; no dilution series |
| **Biological Noise Handling** | Does AI distinguish tumor-derived signals from clonal hematopoiesis? | CHIP variant filtering; white blood cell matched sequencing; age-adjusted analysis | No CHIP filtering; no matched WBC sequencing |
| **Multi-Cancer Detection** | For MCED tests, what is the per-cancer sensitivity? | Per-cancer type sensitivity at defined specificity; stage-stratified sensitivity | Only aggregate sensitivity; no per-cancer breakdown |
| **Tissue-of-Origin Prediction** | Can AI accurately predict the cancer's tissue of origin? | Top-1 and top-2 tissue-of-origin accuracy; per-cancer-type breakdown | Only aggregate origin prediction; no per-type breakdown |
| **Clinical Actionability** | Does a positive result lead to clinically actionable findings? | Positive predictive value; diagnostic workup following positive result; time to diagnosis | No clinical follow-up data; PPV not reported at target prevalence |
| **False Positive Consequence** | What is the clinical impact of false positive results? | Downstream procedure rate; patient anxiety; cost per false positive; overdiagnosis rate | No false positive consequence analysis |

**Liquid Biopsy AI Validation Levels**:

| Level | Name | Requirement | Clinical Implication |
|-------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **LB-V0** | Analytical | LOD established; analytical validation complete | Analytical tool only |
| **LB-V1** | Case-Control | Sensitivity/specificity in case-control study | Overestimates real-world performance |
| **LB-V2** | Prospective Screening | Performance in prospective screening cohort | Real-world PPV established |
| **LB-V3** | Clinical Impact | RCT showing improved outcomes with liquid biopsy-guided care | Clinical benefit demonstrated |
| **LB-V4** | Standard of Care | Integrated into clinical guidelines | Recommended screening tool |

**Rule**: Galleri (MCED test) achieved LB-V2 (prospective screening, PATHFINDER study). It is NOT at LB-V3 (no RCT showing mortality benefit). Do NOT equate detection with mortality reduction.
**Rule**: Liquid biopsy AI MUST report per-cancer-type sensitivity. Aggregate sensitivity is misleading — a test with 90% sensitivity for stage IV cancer but 10% for stage I is NOT useful for early detection.
**Rule**: CHIP (Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential) is the #1 confounder for ctDNA-based cancer detection. Any paper that does not address CHIP filtering is INCOMPLETE.

### Cell Therapy AI Assessment Protocol

When AI is applied to cell therapy design (CAR-T, TCR-T, NK cell therapy):

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Target Antigen Selection** | Does AI select tumor-specific antigens with minimal on-target/off-tumor toxicity? | Antigen expression profiling: tumor vs normal tissue; cross-reactivity screening | Only tumor expression data; no normal tissue profiling |
| **CAR/TCR Design Optimization** | Does AI-optimized CAR/TCR outperform existing designs? | Head-to-head comparison with standard CAR/TCR; functional assay (cytotoxicity, cytokine release) | Only binding affinity reported; no functional assay |
| **Safety Prediction** | Can AI predict cytokine release syndrome (CRS) or neurotoxicity? | Prospective safety prediction; correlation with clinical CRS grade; lead time | Only retrospective CRS association |
| **Manufacturing Optimization** | Does AI improve cell manufacturing yield or quality? | Yield comparison; product consistency; release criteria compliance | Only small-scale manufacturing; no GMP data |
| **Persistence Prediction** | Can AI predict in vivo cell persistence? | Longitudinal cell tracking; correlation with clinical response; durability of remission | Only short-term (<3 month) persistence data |
| **Resistance Mechanism** | Does AI anticipate antigen escape or T-cell exhaustion? | Antigen loss variant analysis; exhaustion marker profiling; escape mechanism modeling | No resistance mechanism analysis |

**Cell Therapy AI Validation Levels**:

| Level | Name | Requirement | Clinical Implication |
|-------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **CT-V0** | In Silico | AI-designed CAR/TCR with computational validation only | Research tool only |
| **CT-V1** | In Vitro | Functional validation in cell-based assays | Proof of concept |
| **CT-V2** | In Vivo | Efficacy + safety in animal model | Preclinical |
| **CT-V3** | Phase I/II | Safety + preliminary efficacy in patients | Clinical feasibility |
| **CT-V4** | Phase III/Approved | Confirmed efficacy in controlled trial | Standard of care candidate |

**Rule**: AI-designed CAR-T that has not been tested for on-target/off-tumor toxicity is at CT-V0 regardless of computational elegance. The history of CAR-T (e.g., ERBB2 CAR-T fatal toxicity) shows that computational target selection is NECESSARY BUT NOT SUFFICIENT.
**Rule**: CRS prediction is the most clinically impactful AI application for CAR-T. A model that predicts severe CRS (grade ≥3) with AUC >0.85 could guide prophylactic tocilizumab use. This is MORE valuable than improving CAR design.
**Rule**: AI for cell therapy manufacturing optimization is UNDERVALUED. Manufacturing consistency is the #1 bottleneck for cell therapy scalability. AI that improves yield by 20% has more clinical impact than AI that improves target selection by 5%.

### Multi-Omics Integration AI Assessment Protocol

When AI integrates multiple omics data types (genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, epigenomics):

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Data Harmonization** | How are different omics data types normalized and harmonized? | Batch effect correction per omics; cross-omics normalization; missing data handling | No batch correction; different omics processed independently then concatenated |
| **Integration Method Justification** | Why is this integration method appropriate for the data? | Method comparison; simulation study; theoretical justification | Default method used without justification; no comparison |
| **Modality Contribution** | What does each omics modality contribute to the final result? | Per-modality ablation; modality-specific feature importance; incremental value analysis | All modalities treated equally; no contribution analysis |
| **Sample Size Adequacy** | Is the sample size adequate for multi-omics integration? | Power analysis for each modality; effective sample size after missing data; curse of dimensionality assessment | n < 10 × number of features; no power analysis |
| **Cross-Platform Reproducibility** | Are results reproducible across different platforms for each omics? | Cross-platform validation per modality; platform-specific bias assessment | Single platform per omics; no cross-platform validation |
| **Biological Coherence** | Do integrated results make biological sense? | Pathway enrichment consistency; known biology validation; expert review | AI finds associations contradicting established biology |

**Multi-Omics Integration Validation Levels**:

| Level | Name | Requirement | Clinical Implication |
|-------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **MO-V0** | Single Cohort | Integration validated on one dataset | Research finding; may not generalize |
| **MO-V1** | Cross-Validation | Robust cross-validation within cohort | Internal validity established |
| **MO-V2** | External Cohort | Validated on independent cohort with same platforms | Generalizability demonstrated |
| **MO-V3** | Cross-Platform | Validated across different omics platforms | Platform-agnostic |
| **MO-V4** | Clinical Utility | Integration improves clinical decision-making | Clinically actionable |

**Rule**: Multi-omics integration with n < 100 samples is almost always UNDERPOWERED. The curse of dimensionality means that with 5 omics × 10,000 features each, you need thousands of samples for reliable integration. Flag: "⚠️ Sample size inadequate for multi-omics integration — high risk of overfitting."
**Rule**: Late integration (analyze each omics separately, then combine results) is often MORE robust than early integration (concatenate all features). Early integration requires much larger sample sizes. The choice must be justified.
**Rule**: Multi-omics AI that does not assess per-modality contribution is INCOMPLETE. If genomics alone achieves 95% of the integrated model's performance, the other modalities add minimal value and increase cost and complexity.

### In Vivo Gene Editing Delivery Assessment Protocol

When evaluating in vivo gene editing delivery systems (LNP, AAV, electroporation, exosomes):

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Delivery Efficiency** | What fraction of target cells receive the editor? | Dose-response curve; target organ editing rate; cell-type-specific editing | Only bulk tissue editing rate; no cell-type resolution |
| **Biodistribution** | Where does the delivery system go beyond the target organ? | Organ-level biodistribution; tissue concentration over time; clearance kinetics | No biodistribution data; only target organ assessed |
| **Immunogenicity** | Does the delivery system trigger immune response? | Anti-drug antibodies; cytokine release; complement activation; repeat-dose immunogenicity | No immunogenicity testing; single-dose only |
| **Tissue Tropism** | Does the delivery system preferentially target the intended tissue? | Tropism profile across organs; targeting ligand validation; off-target organ editing | No tropism data; assumed tissue-specific delivery |
| **Dose Toxicity** | What is the therapeutic index? | MTD (maximum tolerated dose); dose-limiting toxicities; NOAEL | No dose-ranging study; only single dose tested |
| **Durability** | How long does the editing effect persist? | Longitudinal editing rate; protein expression duration; need for re-dosing | Only acute (<1 month) editing data |
| **Manufacturing Consistency** | Is the delivery system reproducibly manufactured? | Batch-to-batch consistency; GMP compliance; release criteria | Only research-grade material; no GMP data |

**Delivery System Comparison**:

| Delivery System | Advantages | Key Risks | Best For |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **LNP** | Non-viral; repeat dosing possible; scalable manufacturing | Liver tropism (default); inflammatory response at high dose; limited extrahepatic targeting | Liver targets (Intellia/Casgevy); mRNA delivery |
| **AAV** | Tissue tropism (serotype-dependent); long-term expression | Pre-existing immunity; limited cargo capacity; integration risk; cannot re-dose | CNS, muscle, retina; DNA delivery |
| **Electroporation** | Ex vivo only; high efficiency; no viral components | Cell viability; limited to accessible cell types | Ex vivo HSC editing (Casgevy) |
| **Exosome** | Natural delivery; low immunogenicity; crosses BBB | Low loading efficiency; unproven at scale | CNS delivery (experimental) |

**Rule**: LNP-based in vivo editing (Intellia 2021, NEJM) demonstrated liver editing but extrahepatic delivery remains a major challenge. Any paper claiming non-liver in vivo editing with standard LNP MUST provide biodistribution evidence.
**Rule**: AAV pre-existing immunity affects 30-60% of the population. Any AAV-based gene editing trial MUST screen for anti-AAV antibodies and report exclusion rates.
**Rule**: The therapeutic index for in vivo gene editing is NARROW. Effective dose and toxic dose may be close. Always assess: "What happens if editing occurs in 2x the target cells?"

### Computational Biomarker Prospective Validation Protocol

When computational biomarkers are validated in prospective clinical studies:

| Validation Stage | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Common Failure Mode |
|-----------------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Pre-Prospective Readiness** | Is the biomarker ready for prospective testing? | Locked-down algorithm; pre-specified analysis plan; power calculation for prospective study | Algorithm still being modified during prospective study |
| **Prospective Accuracy** | Does the biomarker maintain accuracy in prospective use? | Prospective sensitivity/specificity; comparison with retrospective performance | Prospective accuracy 10-30% lower than retrospective |
| **Clinical Decision Impact** | Does the biomarker change clinical decisions? | Concordance rate: biomarker recommendation vs actual clinical action; decision change rate | Biomarker accurate but clinicians ignore it |
| **Patient Outcome Impact** | Does the biomarker improve patient outcomes? | RCT: biomarker-guided care vs standard care; patient-important outcomes | Biomarker changes decisions but not outcomes |
| **Implementation Feasibility** | Can the biomarker be integrated into clinical workflow? | Integration time; computational requirements; workflow disruption analysis | Too slow or disruptive for clinical use |
| **Cost-Effectiveness** | Is biomarker-guided care cost-effective? | ICER per QALY gained; budget impact analysis | Biomarker improves outcomes but at prohibitive cost |

**Computational Biomarker Prospective Validation Levels**:

| Level | Name | Requirement | Clinical Implication |
|-------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **CB-P0** | Retrospective | Validated on historical data only | Research finding |
| **CB-P1** | Prospective-Observational | Prospective data collection; no intervention change | Real-world accuracy established |
| **CB-P2** | Prospective-Interventional | Biomarker influences clinical decisions | Clinical feasibility |
| **CB-P3** | RCT | Randomized comparison: biomarker-guided vs standard care | Clinical benefit demonstrated |
| **CB-P4** | Implementation | Deployed with outcome tracking and continuous monitoring | Standard of care candidate |

**Rule**: EAGLE (Campanella 2025, Nature Medicine) achieved CB-P1 (prospective-observational) for EGFR mutation detection from histology images. The 43% reduction in molecular testing is a CLINICAL EFFICIENCY outcome, not a diagnostic accuracy outcome. Frame appropriately.
**Rule**: The "prospective accuracy drop" is a universal phenomenon. A computational biomarker with 95% retrospective AUROC typically achieves 85-90% prospective AUROC. Plan for this degradation.
**Rule**: A computational biomarker that is accurate but does not change clinical decisions has NO clinical value. Always assess: "What would the clinician do WITHOUT this biomarker?" If the answer is the same, the biomarker is clinically redundant.

### Federated Learning Convergence & Communication Assessment Protocol

When evaluating federated learning systems for medical AI:

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Convergence Guarantee** | Does the FL system reliably converge? | Convergence curve; number of rounds to target performance; convergence failure rate | No convergence analysis; divergent runs excluded |
| **Non-IID Handling** | How does the system handle non-identically distributed data across sites? | Performance under varying data heterogeneity; Dirichlet distribution simulation | Only tested on IID data; no heterogeneity analysis |
| **Communication Efficiency** | What is the bandwidth requirement? | Communication rounds; data transmitted per round; convergence vs communication trade-off | No communication cost analysis; assumes unlimited bandwidth |
| **Straggler Resilience** | How does the system handle slow or unavailable sites? | Performance with varying participation rates; asynchronous vs synchronous comparison | Assumes all sites always available |
| **Cross-Silo vs Cross-Device** | Is the FL architecture appropriate for the deployment scenario? | Architecture justification; site count; data per site | Cross-device architecture used for cross-silo scenario (or vice versa) |
| **Scalability** | How does performance scale with number of sites? | Performance curve: 2 sites → 5 sites → 10 sites → 50 sites | Only tested on 2-3 sites |

**FL Architecture Selection Guide**:

| Scenario | Sites | Data per Site | Recommended Architecture | Key Challenge |
|----------|-------|--------------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Cross-Silo Hospital** | 3-20 | Large (10K-1M) | FedAvg with secure aggregation | Data heterogeneity |
| **Cross-Silo Research** | 5-50 | Medium (1K-100K) | FedProx/FedNova | Non-IID distribution |
| **Cross-Device Wearable** | 100-10K | Small (10-1K) | FedAvgM/Ditto | Communication efficiency |
| **Cross-Device Mobile** | 10K+ | Tiny (1-100) | Federated distillation | Partial participation |

**Rule**: FL papers that only test on 2-3 sites have NOT demonstrated federated learning. The value of FL is in scaling to many sites with heterogeneous data. Test on ≥5 sites with realistic heterogeneity.
**Rule**: FL convergence failure is COMMON in medical settings due to extreme data heterogeneity. Any paper that does not report convergence failure rate is INCOMPLETE.
**Rule**: Communication cost is the PRACTICAL bottleneck for FL deployment. A system that requires 1000 rounds of full model transmission is not deployable in bandwidth-constrained hospital networks.

### Real-World Evidence AI Assessment Protocol

When AI systems are evaluated using real-world evidence (EHR, claims, registry data):

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Data Quality** | Is the real-world data of sufficient quality for AI evaluation? | Completeness; accuracy vs chart review; missingness pattern analysis | No data quality assessment; assumed complete and accurate |
| **Confounding Control** | Are confounders adequately controlled? | Propensity score methods; instrumental variables; negative control outcomes | No confounding adjustment; only covariate adjustment |
| **Label Quality** | Are the outcome labels reliable? | Label validation against chart review; inter-rater agreement; coding accuracy | Labels from billing codes only; no validation |
| **Temporal Validity** | Is the data temporally appropriate? | Index date definition; lookback window; immortal time bias assessment | Immortal time bias; future information leakage |
| **Selection Bias** | Is the study population representative? | Comparison with target population; inclusion/exclusion impact; sensitivity analysis | Convenience sample; no representativeness analysis |
| **Causal Inference** | Can causal claims be made from RWE? | Causal framework specification (PO, DAG); sensitivity to unmeasured confounding | Causal language without causal framework |

**RWE AI Validation Levels**:

| Level | Name | Requirement | Clinical Implication |
|-------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **RWE-V0** | Single-Site Retrospective | EHR data from one institution | Hypothesis-generating only |
| **RWE-V1** | Multi-Site Retrospective | EHR data from multiple institutions | Generalizability improved |
| **RWE-V2** | Prospective Cohort | Prospective data collection with AI running in shadow mode | Real-world accuracy |
| **RWE-V3** | Pragmatic Trial | Pragmatic RCT using RWE infrastructure | Clinical effectiveness |
| **RWE-V4** | Regulatory Grade | RWE meeting FDA/EMA standards for regulatory submission | Supports regulatory claims |

**Rule**: Billing code-based labels (ICD codes) have 60-85% positive predictive value for many conditions. AI trained on billing codes inherits this label noise. Flag: "⚠️ Label quality: ICD codes have PPV 60-85% — AI accuracy ceiling is limited by label quality."
**Rule**: Immortal time bias is the #1 methodological error in RWE AI studies. If the AI requires data from time T1 to make predictions, but the cohort is defined at time T0 < T1, patients who die between T0 and T1 are excluded → immortal time bias.
**Rule**: RWE cannot replace RCTs for efficacy claims. RWE is for EFFECTIVENESS (does it work in practice?), not EFFICACY (does it work in ideal conditions?). Do not conflate the two.

### AI Model Robustness & Adversarial Assessment Protocol

When evaluating the robustness of medical AI systems:

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Distribution Shift** | How does performance change with new data distributions? | Performance on external datasets; temporal validation; geographic validation | Only internal validation; no distribution shift analysis |
| **Adversarial Robustness** | Is the model robust to adversarial perturbations? | Adversarial attack success rate; perturbation magnitude; defense effectiveness | No adversarial robustness testing |
| **Out-of-Distribution Detection** | Does the model know when it encounters unfamiliar inputs? | OOD detection AUROC; selective prediction on OOD samples; calibration on OOD data | No OOD detection; model always produces confident predictions |
| **Natural Perturbation Robustness** | Is the model robust to realistic clinical variations? | Performance under: noise, resolution change, device change, patient position change | Only tested on clean, standardized data |
| **Subgroup Robustness** | Is performance consistent across patient subgroups? | Per-subgroup performance; worst-subgroup performance; intersectional analysis | Only aggregate performance reported |
| **Failure Mode Analysis** | What are the systematic failure modes? | Error analysis; failure pattern identification; clinical consequence of failures | No failure mode analysis |

**Medical AI Adversarial Threat Model**:

| Threat | Realism | Impact | Assessment Priority |
|--------|---------|--------|-------------------|
| **Natural Distribution Shift** | Very High | Moderate-High | CRITICAL — must test |
| **Device/Protocol Variation** | High | Moderate | HIGH — should test |
| **Demographic Shift** | High | High | HIGH — should test |
| **Intentional Adversarial Attack** | Low | Very High | MODERATE — awareness needed |
| **Data Poisoning** | Low-Moderate | High | MODERATE — for training pipeline |

**Rule**: For medical AI, NATURAL distribution shift is a MUCH bigger threat than adversarial attacks. Prioritize testing on: (1) different hospitals, (2) different patient populations, (3) different devices, (4) different time periods. Adversarial attack testing is secondary.
**Rule**: A medical AI that is 99% accurate on average but 70% accurate on a specific subgroup is NOT robust. Worst-subgroup performance is MORE important than average performance for clinical deployment.
**Rule**: OOD detection is ESSENTIAL for clinical AI. A model that confidently predicts on inputs it has never seen is DANGEROUS. Flag: "⚠️ No OOD detection — model may produce confident wrong predictions on unfamiliar inputs."

### AI Model Versioning & Reproducibility Protocol

When evaluating the reproducibility and versioning of medical AI systems:

| Assessment Dimension | Key Questions | Required Evidence | Red Flag |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Model Versioning** | Is the model version explicitly tracked? | Model version ID; change log; performance per version | No version tracking; "latest model" used |
| **Data Versioning** | Is the training data version tracked? | Data snapshot ID; preprocessing pipeline version; feature version | Data not versioned; preprocessing not documented |
| **Code Reproducibility** | Can the training code reproduce the reported results? | Fixed random seeds; deterministic training; containerized environment | No seed fixing; non-deterministic training |
| **Performance Stability** | Is performance stable across retraining runs? | Performance variance across runs; confidence intervals; retraining agreement | Only single training run reported |
| **Update Protocol** | How are model updates managed and validated? | Update trigger criteria; validation before deployment; rollback mechanism | No update protocol; ad-hoc updates |
| **Audit Trail** | Is there a complete audit trail for clinical decisions? | Input logging; model version logging; output logging; human override logging | No audit trail; cannot trace clinical decisions to specific model version |

**Model Reproducibility Checklist**:

| Item | Required | Common Failure |
|------|----------|---------------|
| **Code available** | ✅ Mandatory | Code not shared or incomplete |
| **Data available** | ✅ Mandatory (or synthetic equivalent) | Proprietary data; no access |
| **Environment specified** | ✅ Mandatory | Missing library versions; GPU dependency |
| **Random seed fixed** | ✅ Mandatory | Non-reproducible results across runs |
| **Hyperparameters documented** | ✅ Mandatory | Only final hyperparameters; no search process |
| **Preprocessing pipeline versioned** | ✅ Mandatory | Preprocessing changes not tracked |
| **Model checkpoint available** | ✅ Recommended | Cannot reproduce exact model state |
| **Training curve reported** | ✅ Recommended | Only final performance; no convergence evidence |

**Rule**: Medical AI models without version control are NOT deployable. If you cannot identify which model version made a specific prediction, you cannot investigate errors or ensure accountability.
**Rule**: "We retrained the model and performance improved" without specifying what changed is MEANINGLESS. The improvement may be due to data update, code fix, hyperparameter change, or random variation. All changes must be documented.
**Rule**: FDA's "predetermined change control plan" requires specifying in advance what changes will be made and how they will be validated. Uncontrolled model updates = regulatory violation.

### Counterfactual Clinical Reasoning Protocol

When evaluating a research claim, apply counterfactual reasoning BEFORE consulting literature:

| Reasoning Step | Question | Method | Output |
|---------------|----------|--------|--------|
| **1. Claim Decomposition** | What exactly is being claimed? | Identify: input signal, output prediction, claimed mechanism, claimed performance | Precise claim statement |
| **2. Null Hypothesis Construction** | If this claim were FALSE, what would we observe? | Construct the most plausible alternative explanation | Competing hypothesis |
| **3. Discriminating Prediction** | What observation would distinguish the claim from the null? | Identify a prediction that the claim makes but the null does not | Critical test |
| **4. Prior Probability Assessment** | How surprising is this claim given what we know? | Assess: Does this contradict established biology? Is the mechanism novel or established? | Prior probability (high/medium/low) |
| **5. Evidence Weight Assessment** | How much evidence would be needed to shift belief? | Extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence; incremental claims need standard evidence | Evidence threshold |
| **6. Alternative Explanation Check** | What is the most likely alternative explanation? | Shortcut detection; confound analysis; methodological artifact | Alternative explanation list |

**Counterfactual Reasoning Examples**:

| Claim | Null Hypothesis | Discriminating Prediction | Prior Probability | Evidence Needed |
|-------|----------------|--------------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| "AI detects liver cirrhosis from ECG" | AI detects treatment effects (beta-blockers) that correlate with cirrhosis | AI works on treatment-naive cirrhosis patients | Medium (known ECG changes in cirrhosis) | Prospective validation on untreated patients |
| "AI predicts cancer from blood draw" | AI detects existing cancer signals (ctDNA) that are already known | AI predicts cancer BEFORE any molecular signal is detectable | Low (no known pre-cancer blood signal) | Longitudinal prediction years before diagnosis |
| "LLM reasons about medical cases" | LLM pattern-matches from training data | LLM performs well on novel case combinations not in training | Low (JAMA 2025 evidence against) | Novel combination test + counterfactual test |
| "AI detects depression from voice" | AI detects medication side effects affecting voice | AI works on unmedicated depression patients | Medium (voice changes in depression known) | Validation excluding medicated patients |

**Rule**: ALWAYS construct the null hypothesis BEFORE evaluating evidence. If you look at evidence first, you will rationalize it to fit the claim (confirmation bias).
**Rule**: If the discriminating prediction has NOT been tested, the claim is UNVERIFIED regardless of how impressive the reported results are.
**Rule**: The most dangerous claims are those with MEDIUM prior probability — they are plausible enough to seem credible but novel enough to be wrong. Apply the STRICTEST scrutiny to medium-prior-probability claims.

### Biological Plausibility First Principles Reasoning Protocol

When assessing biological plausibility of a novel claim, reason from first principles WITHOUT literature lookup:

| Reasoning Level | Question | Method | Example |
|----------------|----------|--------|---------|
| **L1: Physical Law** | Does the claim violate physical laws? | Check conservation laws, thermodynamics, information theory | "AI detects disease from quantum energy field" → violates no known physical mechanism |
| **L2: Biological Principle** | Does the claim violate established biological principles? | Check: central dogma, homeostasis, known pathways | "AI reverses aging from ECG" → aging is multi-system; ECG cannot reverse it |
| **L3: Physiological Pathway** | Is there a known physiological pathway connecting input to output? | Trace: signal → organ system → condition | ECG → cardiac electrophysiology → arrhythmia (direct); ECG → liver function (indirect via hemodynamics) |
| **L4: Information Content** | Does the input signal contain sufficient information? | Calculate: Shannon information in signal vs information needed for prediction | ECG has ~1000 bits/beat; arrhythmia classification needs ~10 bits → FEASIBLE |
| **L5: Temporal Consistency** | Is the temporal relationship between input and output consistent? | Check: does the signal change BEFORE or AFTER the condition? | ECG changes during ischemia (simultaneous) → detection feasible; ECG changes before cancer (no mechanism) → detection implausible |
| **L6: Evolutionary Argument** | Would evolution have created or eliminated this signal-condition link? | Consider: is the link adaptive, neutral, or maladaptive? | Skin color changes in jaundice (bilirubin deposition) → evolutionarily neutral → signal exists |

**Biological Plausibility Decision Tree**:

```
Claim: "AI detects condition X from signal Y"
│
├─ Does signal Y have physical contact/information pathway to condition X?
│   ├─ YES → Plausible at L3; proceed to L4
│   └─ NO → Is there an indirect pathway (≥2 steps)?
│       ├─ YES → Weakly plausible; each step attenuates signal
│       └─ NO → Implausible at L3; flag "⚠️ No known biological pathway"
│
├─ Does signal Y contain sufficient information content?
│   ├─ YES → Information-theoretically feasible
│   └─ NO → Flag "⚠️ Information content insufficient — may be detecting confound"
│
├─ Is the temporal relationship consistent?
│   ├─ Signal BEFORE condition → Predictive (strong claim, needs strong evidence)
│   ├─ Signal DURING condition → Diagnostic (moderate claim, standard evidence)
│   └─ Signal AFTER condition → Retrospective (weak claim, easy to detect)
│
└─ Overall assessment:
    ├─ All levels pass → BIOLOGICALLY PLAUSIBLE; proceed to empirical validation
    ├─ L1-L2 fail → BIOLOGICALLY IMPLAUSIBLE; flag as likely artifact
    ├─ L3 fails but L4 passes → POSSIBLE BUT UNEXPLAINED; flag for mechanism research
    └─ L4 fails but L3 passes → MECHANISM EXISTS BUT SIGNAL TOO WEAK; flag for sensitivity analysis
```

**Rule**: If a claim fails at L1 or L2, it is almost certainly wrong. Do not waste time on empirical validation — the claim violates fundamental principles.
**Rule**: If a claim passes L1-L3 but fails L4, the AI may be detecting a confound rather than the claimed signal. This is the most common failure mode for "AI detects X from Y" papers.
**Rule**: The information content analysis (L4) is QUANTIFIABLE. If the signal has fewer bits of information than the prediction requires, the claim is information-theoretically impossible.

### Clinical Workflow Integration Reasoning Protocol

When evaluating whether an AI system can be integrated into clinical practice:

| Reasoning Dimension | Key Questions | Analysis Method | Common Failure |
|-------------------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Decision Point Mapping** | At which point in the clinical workflow would AI intervene? | Map: patient journey → decision points → AI insertion point | AI designed for a decision point that doesn't exist in practice |
| **Information Availability** | Is the required input data available at the decision point? | Check: what data exists when the decision is made? | AI requires lab results that haven't been ordered yet |
| **Time Constraint** | Does the AI respond within the clinical decision window? | Measure: AI latency vs clinical decision timeline | AI takes 30 min but clinician needs answer in 5 min |
| **Cognitive Load** | Does the AI reduce or increase clinician cognitive load? | Assess: alert fatigue, information overload, workflow disruption | AI generates too many alerts → clinicians ignore all alerts |
| **Liability Allocation** | Who is responsible when the AI is wrong? | Legal analysis: manufacturer vs clinician vs hospital | Unclear liability → clinicians refuse to use AI |
| **Reimbursement** | Is there a billing code for the AI service? | Check: CPT codes, ICD-10, payer policies | No reimbursement pathway → hospital won't adopt |
| **Competing Alternative** | What is the clinician already doing at this decision point? | Compare: AI vs current standard practice | AI is slower/less accurate than current practice |

**Clinical Workflow Integration Levels**:

| Level | Name | Requirement | Clinical Implication |
|-------|------|------------|---------------------|
| **WI-0** | Conceptual | AI works technically but no workflow analysis | Not deployable |
| **WI-1** | Shadow Mode | AI runs alongside workflow without affecting decisions | Safe for evaluation |
| **WI-2** | Decision Support | AI provides recommendations within existing workflow | Clinician accepts or rejects |
| **WI-3** | Workflow Embedded | AI is part of the standard clinical workflow | Required for patient care |
| **WI-4** | Workflow Defining | AI defines the clinical workflow | Standard of care |

**Rule**: An AI system that is technically excellent but workflow-incompatible is CLINICALLY USELESS. Technical accuracy is NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT for clinical impact.
**Rule**: The #1 reason AI systems fail in clinical deployment is NOT technical inadequacy — it is WORKFLOW INCOMPATIBILITY. Always assess workflow integration BEFORE assessing technical performance.
**Rule**: Alert fatigue is the silent killer of clinical AI. If the AI generates >10 alerts per shift, clinicians will ignore it. Design for SPECIFICITY, not sensitivity, in clinical deployment.

### Knowledge Structure-Driven Research Gap Identification Protocol

When identifying research gaps from the structure of existing knowledge (not from literature search):

| Gap Type | Identification Method | Reasoning Process | Example |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Missing Link** | Two known facts exist but the connection is unstudied | Fact A → [GAP] → Fact B; what connects them? | "ECG detects arrhythmia" + "Sleep apnea causes arrhythmia" → Can ECG detect sleep apnea? |
| **Method Transfer** | Method works in domain A but untested in domain B | Method M solves problem P_A; does an analogous problem P_B exist? | "CNN classifies images" → "Can CNN classify spectrograms?" (done) → "Can CNN classify genomic profiles as images?" (emerging) |
| **Scale Gap** | Method works at small scale but untested at large scale | Method M works for n=100; what happens at n=100,000? | "FL works for 3 hospitals" → "Does FL work for 50 hospitals with extreme heterogeneity?" |
| **Composition Gap** | Components A and B exist but A+B is unstudied | Component A + Component B → emergent behavior? | "LLM reads papers" + "AI designs experiments" → "LLM designs experiments from papers?" |
| **Assumption Violation** | Method assumes X but in practice not-X | What if the core assumption is violated? | "Training data is representative" → "What if disease prevalence shifts?" |
| **Temporal Gap** | Method validated at time T but conditions change over time | What happens as the underlying data distribution evolves? | "AI validated in 2023" → "Does AI still work in 2026 after clinical practice changes?" |

**Gap Prioritization Matrix**:

| Gap Type | Impact (if filled) | Feasibility | Priority | Example |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|----------|---------|
| **Missing Link** | High (new connection) | Medium (needs new data) | HIGH | ECG → sleep apnea connection |
| **Method Transfer** | Medium (known method, new domain) | High (method exists) | HIGH | Genomic profile as image |
| **Scale Gap** | Medium (validates generalizability) | Medium (needs more sites) | MEDIUM | FL at 50 hospitals |
| **Composition Gap** | Very High (emergent capability) | Low (complex integration) | MEDIUM-HIGH | LLM + experiment design |
| **Assumption Violation** | High (reveals fragility) | High (can test with existing data) | HIGH | Distribution shift testing |
| **Temporal Gap** | Medium (maintains relevance) | Medium (needs longitudinal data) | MEDIUM | AI drift over time |

**Rule**: The most valuable research gaps are MISSING LINKS and COMPOSITION GAPS — they have the highest potential for genuine innovation.
**Rule**: Research gaps identified from knowledge structure are MORE RELIABLE than gaps identified from literature search, because they are based on LOGICAL NECESSITY (the connection MUST exist) rather than ABSENCE (nobody has published on it yet).
**Rule**: When identifying gaps, always ask: "If this gap were filled, would it change clinical practice?" If not, the gap is academically interesting but not practically important.

### Cross-Domain Analogy Reasoning Protocol

When reasoning by analogy across domains to solve problems or evaluate claims:

| Analogy Step | Description | Method | Validation |
|-------------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **1. Source Domain Identification** | Find a domain where a similar problem has been solved | Structural comparison: what is the mathematical/structural similarity? | Similarity must be STRUCTURAL, not superficial |
| **2. Mapping Verification** | Verify that the structural mapping is valid | Check: do the same constraints apply? Are the key variables analogous? | If constraints differ, analogy may be invalid |
| **3. Solution Transfer** | Transfer the solution from source to target domain | Adapt the source solution to target domain constraints | Adaptation must preserve the key insight |
| **4. Failure Mode Transfer** | Transfer known failure modes from source domain | Check: do the failure modes in the source domain have analogs in the target? | Failure modes may not transfer if constraints differ |
| **5. Validation Requirement Transfer** | Transfer validation requirements from source domain | Check: what validation level was required in the source domain? | Target domain may require HIGHER validation (e.g., clinical vs engineering) |

**Cross-Domain Analogy Map for Biomedical AI**:

| Source Domain | Target Domain | Structural Similarity | Key Difference | Transferred Insight |
|--------------|--------------|----------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Autonomous Driving** | Autonomous Surgery | Sequential decision-making under uncertainty; safety-critical | Human body is more variable than roads; no "test track" | Safety architecture: redundant systems, graceful degradation |
| **NLP Translation** | Protein Sequence Design | Sequence-to-sequence mapping; attention mechanism | Protein folding physics ≠ language grammar | Transformer architecture for sequence design |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Clinical Decision Support | Personalized prediction from historical data | Medical errors are irreversible; recommendation errors are not | Collaborative filtering → similar patient outcomes |
| **Fraud Detection** | Rare Disease Diagnosis | Imbalanced classification; anomaly detection | Fraud is adversarial; rare disease is not | Anomaly detection methods; cost-sensitive learning |
| **Weather Prediction** | Disease Outbreak Prediction | Spatiotemporal forecasting; chaotic dynamics | Weather has better physical models; disease has behavioral factors | Ensemble forecasting; uncertainty quantification |
| **Image Search** | Medical Image Retrieval | Feature extraction; similarity search | Medical images have stricter privacy requirements | Hashing-based retrieval; privacy-preserving search |

**Rule**: Analogies are HEURISTICS, not proofs. A solution that works in the source domain may fail in the target domain due to constraint differences. Always validate the transferred solution.
**Rule**: The most dangerous analogy error is SURFACE SIMILARITY without STRUCTURAL SIMILARITY. "AI drives cars → AI can do surgery" is surface-level; the structural similarity is in sequential decision-making under uncertainty, not in "AI controlling things."
**Rule**: When transferring validation requirements, ALWAYS apply the STRICTEST standard. If autonomous driving requires 1 billion miles of testing, autonomous surgery may need an analogous level of evidence — but what constitutes "1 billion miles" in surgery?

### System Architecture-Driven Failure Prediction Protocol

When predicting failure modes from system architecture alone (without observing actual failures):

| Architecture Pattern | Predicted Failure Mode | Reasoning | Preventive Measure |
|---------------------|----------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Cascade Architecture** (A→B→C) | Error amplification: small error in A becomes large error in C | Each stage multiplies error; no error correction between stages | Add error checking at each stage; confidence thresholds |
| **Ensemble Architecture** (A+B+C→vote) | Disagreement at boundaries: ensemble members disagree on edge cases | Members agree on easy cases but disagree on hard cases | Report disagreement rate; use disagreement as uncertainty signal |
| **Feedback Loop Architecture** (A→B→A) | Runaway feedback: small perturbation amplified through loop | Positive feedback without damping → instability | Add damping; limit feedback gain; monitor for oscillation |
| **Black Box + Post-hoc Explanation** | Unfaithful explanation: explanation does not reflect actual reasoning | Post-hoc methods approximate but don't reveal mechanism | Use inherently interpretable models; validate explanation faithfulness |
| **Transfer Learning Architecture** (Pretrain→Finetune) | Negative transfer: pretrained features hurt target task | Source and target domains are too different | Measure transferability; compare with training from scratch |
| **Multi-Task Architecture** (Shared→Task-specific) | Task interference: optimizing for task A hurts task B | Shared representation cannot serve conflicting objectives | Report per-task performance; use task-specific heads with shared backbone |
| **Federated Architecture** (Local→Global→Local) | Aggregation bias: global model works for dominant sites only | Averaging favors high-data sites; minority sites underrepresented | Per-site evaluation; personalized fine-tuning |
| **Agent Architecture** (LLM→Tools→LLM) | Tool failure propagation: wrong tool output → wrong LLM reasoning | LLM cannot verify tool output quality; trusts tool blindly | Tool output validation; cross-tool consistency check |

**Architecture Risk Scoring**:

| Risk Factor | Score | Description |
|------------|-------|-------------|
| **Cascade depth** | +1 per stage | Each cascade stage adds error amplification risk |
| **No error correction** | +2 | No mechanism to detect/correct intermediate errors |
| **Feedback loop** | +3 | Positive feedback without damping |
| **Black box component** | +1 | Cannot inspect intermediate reasoning |
| **Cross-domain transfer** | +2 | Source-target domain gap |
| **Multi-objective conflict** | +1 | Competing objectives without explicit trade-off |
| **External dependency** | +1 | Relies on external tool/API without validation |

**Rule**: Architecture risk score ≥5 = HIGH RISK. The system is likely to fail in ways not captured by standard testing. Apply additional stress testing.
**Rule**: The most dangerous architecture is CASCADE + FEEDBACK LOOP (risk score ≥6). Small errors amplify through the cascade and then feed back, creating exponential error growth. This architecture should be AVOIDED in safety-critical systems.
**Rule**: When reviewing a paper, ALWAYS draw the system architecture diagram and identify the failure modes BEFORE reading the results. This prevents hindsight bias — you will see whether the authors tested the failure modes you predicted.

### Six-Component Integration Protocol

The skill's six core components must operate as an integrated system, not as isolated modules:

| Component | Role | Input | Output | Dependency |
|-----------|------|-------|--------|-----------|
| **1. Automatic Learning** | Absorb new knowledge from testing outcomes | Testing results, calibration signals | Updated baselines, new rules | Receives input from Component 3 |
| **2. Automatic Evolution** | Self-modify framework based on accumulated learning | Learning outputs, trigger events | New/modified protocols, rule changes | Receives input from Component 1 |
| **3. Continuous Testing** | Systematically evaluate framework against real/virtual data | Test cases (core journals, directions, experiments) | Defect reports, calibration signals | Receives input from Component 7 |
| **4. Substantive Skill System** | Provide domain expertise beyond database matching | User queries, paper inputs | Expert analysis, independent reasoning | Receives input from Components 1, 2, 7 |
| **5. Virtual Experiment Simulation** | Test hypotheses and design experiments without real data | Research questions, hypotheses | Experiment designs, predicted outcomes | Receives input from Component 4 |
| **6. Database Matching Analysis** | Match against known literature and methods | Paper features, method signatures | Literature matches, method references | Provides input to Components 3, 4 |
| **7. Analytical Framework** | Provide reasoning structure for all components | Domain knowledge, reasoning protocols | Reasoning chains, plausibility assessments | Provides input to ALL components |

**Integration Flow**:

```
User Query / Paper Input
        │
        ▼
[6. Database Matching] ──→ Literature matches, method references
        │
        ▼
[7. Analytical Framework] ──→ Reasoning chains, plausibility checks
        │
        ▼
[4. Substantive Skill System] ──→ Expert analysis, independent reasoning
        │                           │
        │                           ▼
        │              [5. Virtual Experiment] ──→ Experiment designs
        │
        ▼
[3. Continuous Testing] ──→ Defect reports, calibration signals
        │
        ▼
[1. Automatic Learning] ──→ Updated baselines, new rules
        │
        ▼
[2. Automatic Evolution] ──→ Framework modifications
        │
        └──────→ Feeds back to ALL components
```

**Rule**: ALL six components must be active for the skill to function correctly. If any component is missing or degraded, the entire system's capability is compromised.
**Rule**: The Analytical Framework (Component 7) is the ORCHESTRATOR — it ensures that database matching results are critically evaluated, not blindly accepted.
**Rule**: Database Matching (Component 6) is a RESOURCE, not the brain. It provides raw material that the Analytical Framework must process, validate, and integrate.

### Evolution Trigger Protocol

Framework evolution should be triggered by BOTH time-based and event-based signals:

| Trigger Type | Condition | Evolution Action | Priority |
|-------------|-----------|-----------------|----------|
| **Time-Based** | 6 months elapsed | Refresh frontier predictions | Scheduled |
| **Time-Based** | 12 months elapsed | Update baseline reference values | Scheduled |
| **Time-Based** | 24 months elapsed | Review innovation taxonomy | Scheduled |
| **Calibration Signal** | 3+ over-optimistic assessments in same domain | Tighten domain-specific criteria | HIGH |
| **Calibration Signal** | 3+ over-conservative assessments in same domain | Loosen domain-specific criteria | HIGH |
| **New Landmark Paper** | Paper achieves L4+ innovation with V3+ validation | Add domain-specific rule; update frontier stage | CRITICAL |
| **Retraction Cascade** | Multiple papers in a sub-field retracted | Add field-specific integrity check | CRITICAL |
| **Regulatory Change** | FDA/EMA issues new AI guidance | Update regulatory compliance protocol | HIGH |
| **Technology Shift** | New paradigm (e.g., diffusion models replace GANs) | Update method selection guide; add new assessment protocol | HIGH |
| **Gap Discovery** | Testing reveals systematic gap in framework coverage | Add new assessment protocol | MODERATE |
| **Conflict Discovery** | Self-critical rule resolution identifies new conflict | Add specific resolution; update hierarchy | MODERATE |
| **Cross-Domain Transfer Success** | Method from domain A succeeds in domain B | Update cross-domain analogy map; add transfer reasoning | LOW |

**Evolution Decision Tree**:

```
Trigger detected
│
├─ Is this a CRITICAL trigger (landmark paper / retraction cascade)?
│   ├─ YES → Immediate evolution; add new rule/protocol; re-test affected assessments
│   └─ NO → Continue
│
├─ Is this a HIGH trigger (calibration signal / regulatory change / technology shift)?
│   ├─ YES → Schedule evolution within 1 week; batch related changes
│   └─ NO → Continue
│
├─ Is this a MODERATE trigger (gap/conflict discovery)?
│   ├─ YES → Add to evolution backlog; batch with next scheduled refresh
│   └─ NO → Continue
│
└─ LOW trigger → Add to evolution backlog; address during next 6-month refresh
```

**Rule**: CRITICAL triggers must be addressed IMMEDIATELY — they indicate the framework is producing wrong assessments. Delay = continued wrong assessments.
**Rule**: Evolution changes must be VALIDATED before deployment. A new rule that fixes one problem may create others. Test against the same papers that triggered the evolution.
**Rule**: Every evolution must be LOGGED with: trigger, change, validation result, rollback plan. If the evolution causes new problems, rollback must be possible.

### Cross-Component Consistency Verification Protocol

When adding a new protocol or modifying an existing one, verify consistency across ALL components:

| Verification Step | What to Check | Method | Failure Action |
|-----------------|--------------|--------|---------------|
| **1. Rule Conflict Check** | Does the new rule conflict with existing rules? | Search all files for rules on the same topic; apply Self-Critical Rule Resolution Protocol | Resolve conflict using priority hierarchy before deployment |
| **2. Validation Level Consistency** | Are validation levels consistent across protocols? | Compare validation level definitions (V0-V5, CS-V0-V4, etc.); ensure same evidence standard | Harmonize validation level definitions |
| **3. Baseline Consistency** | Are baseline reference values consistent across protocols? | Cross-check: does the same metric have different baselines in different protocols? | Use the MOST CONSERVATIVE baseline |
| **4. Terminology Consistency** | Are terms used consistently across protocols? | Search for same term with different definitions; identify ambiguous terms | Add disambiguation note; standardize definition |
| **5. Coverage Completeness** | Does the new protocol create gaps in coverage? | Check: are there scenarios not covered by any protocol? | Add gap-filling protocol or extend existing one |
| **6. Redundancy Check** | Is the new protocol redundant with existing ones? | Compare scope and assessment dimensions; identify overlap | Merge overlapping protocols; clarify distinct scope |

**Common Consistency Failures**:

| Failure Type | Example | Detection Method | Fix |
|-------------|---------|-----------------|-----|
| **Conflicting Thresholds** | Protocol A says AUROC >0.9 is "excellent"; Protocol B says >0.85 is "excellent" | Search all protocols for threshold definitions | Standardize: use the STRICTER threshold |
| **Overlapping Scope** | AI-ECG protocol and Multi-Disease Calibration protocol both cover ECG cross-disease | Check: same scenario covered by two protocols | Specify which protocol takes precedence |
| **Missing Bridge** | Gene Editing Safety protocol doesn't reference In Vivo Delivery protocol | Check: do related protocols reference each other? | Add cross-reference |
| **Divergent Validation Levels** | One protocol uses V0-V4; another uses V0-V5 | Check: are validation level definitions aligned? | Align to common framework; add domain-specific extensions |

**Rule**: Every new protocol MUST pass the 6-step consistency verification before deployment. Unverified protocols may create contradictions that undermine the entire framework.
**Rule**: When two protocols cover overlapping territory, the MORE SPECIFIC protocol takes precedence. The LESS SPECIFIC protocol provides general guidance.
**Rule**: Validation levels across all protocols should be HARMONIZED. V0 = research only; V1 = proof of concept; V2 = validated; V3 = clinical; V4 = standard of care. Domain-specific extensions (e.g., CS-V0-V4 for AI-ECG) should map to this common framework.

### Skill Self-Assessment Protocol

The framework must periodically assess its own capabilities and limitations:

| Self-Assessment Dimension | Assessment Method | Success Criterion | Failure Action |
|--------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Coverage Completeness** | Test against 100 diverse papers from core journals | ≥90% of papers can be assessed using existing protocols | Identify uncovered scenarios; add protocols |
| **Assessment Accuracy** | Compare framework assessments with expert consensus | ≥80% agreement with expert panel on innovation level | Calibrate scoring criteria |
| **Reasoning Independence** | Test: can the framework reason about novel scenarios without database matches? | ≥70% of novel scenarios receive plausible reasoning chains | Strengthen first-principles reasoning protocols |
| **Consistency** | Test: do repeated assessments of the same paper produce the same result? | ≥90% agreement across repeated assessments | Reduce ambiguity in rules |
| **Calibration** | Test: do framework predictions match real-world outcomes? | ≥75% of predictions confirmed within 2 years | Update prediction model |
| **Evolution Rate** | Measure: how often does the framework need updates? | <10% of rules changed per 6-month cycle | If >10%, framework may be unstable |
| **Gap Detection Rate** | Measure: what fraction of real-world gaps does the framework identify? | ≥60% of known gaps identified proactively | Strengthen gap identification protocols |

**Self-Assessment Schedule**:

| Frequency | Scope | Method |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| **Every testing round** | Quick check: any calibration signals? | Review defect reports for patterns |
| **Every 10 testing rounds** | Medium check: coverage and consistency | Test against 10 diverse papers; check for gaps |
| **Every 50 testing rounds** | Full check: all 7 dimensions | Comprehensive self-assessment; expert comparison if available |
| **After CRITICAL evolution** | Targeted check: verify evolution didn't break anything | Re-test papers that triggered the evolution |

**Rule**: Self-assessment is NOT optional — it is the mechanism that ensures the framework remains calibrated and reliable. Without self-assessment, the framework will drift.
**Rule**: The most important self-assessment dimension is REASONING INDEPENDENCE. If the framework can only reason about scenarios it has seen before, it is database matching, not genuine expertise.
**Rule**: A framework that changes >10% of its rules per cycle is UNSTABLE. Stability is a feature — frequent changes indicate the framework is not well-founded.

### Knowledge Graph Integration Protocol

All assessment protocols are connected through a unified knowledge graph:

**Core Knowledge Graph Structure**:

```
                        ┌─────────────────────┐
                        │  RESEARCH CLAIM      │
                        │  (input to framework) │
                        └──────────┬──────────┘
                                   │
                    ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
                    │              │              │
              ┌─────▼─────┐ ┌─────▼─────┐ ┌─────▼─────┐
              │ BIOLOGICAL │ │ METHOD    │ │ CLINICAL  │
              │ PLAUSIBILITY│ │ FIT      │ │ WORKFLOW  │
              │ CHECK      │ │ CHECK    │ │ CHECK     │
              └─────┬─────┘ └─────┬─────┘ └─────┬─────┘
                    │              │              │
                    └──────────────┼──────────────┘
                                   │
                        ┌──────────▼──────────┐
                        │  INNOVATION          │
                        │  ASSESSMENT (L1-L5)  │
                        └──────────┬──────────┘
                                   │
                    ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
                    │              │              │
              ┌─────▼─────┐ ┌─────▼─────┐ ┌─────▼─────┐
              │ FRONTIER   │ │ VALIDATION│ │ SAFETY    │
              │ DETECTION  │ │ LEVEL     │ │ ASSESSMENT│
              │ (7 signals)│ │ (V0-V5)   │ │           │
              └─────┬─────┘ └─────┬─────┘ └─────┬─────┘
                    │              │              │
                    └──────────────┼──────────────┘
                                   │
                        ┌──────────▼──────────┐
                        │  DOMAIN-SPECIFIC     │
                        │  ASSESSMENT          │
                        │  (select by domain)  │
                        └──────────┬──────────┘
                                   │
           ┌───────────┬───────────┼───────────┬───────────┐
           │           │           │           │           │
     ┌─────▼────┐┌─────▼────┐┌─────▼────┐┌─────▼────┐┌─────▼────┐
     │ AI-ECG   ││ Drug     ││ Digital  ││ BCI/     ││ Surgery  │
     │ Protocol ││ Discovery││ Twin     ││ Neural   ││ Protocol │
     │          ││ Protocol ││ Protocol ││ Protocol ││          │
     └──────────┘└──────────┘└──────────┘└──────────┘└──────────┘
           │           │           │           │           │
     ┌─────▼────┐┌─────▼────┐┌─────▼────┐┌─────▼────┐┌─────▼────┐
     │ Liquid   ││ Cell     ││ Spatial  ││ Multi-   ││ RWE      │
     │ Biopsy   ││ Therapy  ││ Biology  ││ Omics    ││ Protocol │
     │ Protocol ││ Protocol ││ Protocol ││ Protocol ││          │
     └──────────┘└──────────┘└──────────┘└──────────┘└──────────┘
                                   │
                        ┌──────────▼──────────┐
                        │  OUTPUT              │
                        │  Assessment +        │
                        │  Recommendations     │
                        └─────────────────────┘
```

**Protocol Selection Rules**:

| If paper involves... | Load these protocols | Priority order |
|---------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **AI + ECG + non-cardiac condition** | AI-ECG Cross-Disease + Multi-Disease Calibration | AI-ECG first (more specific) |
| **AI + drug discovery + clinical trial** | Drug Discovery Pipeline + AI-Optimized Trial Design + Clinical Translation | Pipeline first (upstream) |
| **AI + digital twin + clinical deployment** | Digital Twin Validation + Digital Twin Deployment + Clinical Workflow | Validation first (must pass before deployment) |
| **AI + BCI + long-term use** | BCI Long-Term Stability + BCI Home-Use + Robustness Assessment | Stability first (must be stable before home use) |
| **AI + spatial biology + clinical** | Spatial Biology AI + Multi-Omics Integration + Clinical Workflow | Spatial first (data quality gate) |
| **AI + surgery + autonomy** | AI Surgery + Robustness Assessment + Safety Assessment | Safety first (highest priority) |
| **AI + liquid biopsy + screening** | Liquid Biopsy AI + AI Screening Overdiagnosis + Clinical Workflow | Liquid Biopsy first (analytical validity gate) |
| **AI + cell therapy + safety** | Cell Therapy AI + Gene Editing Safety + In Vivo Delivery | Safety first (highest priority) |
| **AI + multi-omics + clinical** | Multi-Omics Integration + Spatial Biology + Clinical Workflow | Integration first (data quality gate) |
| **AI + federated learning** | FL Convergence + FL Bias Propagation + FL Security | Security first (privacy is non-negotiable) |

**Rule**: When multiple protocols apply, ALWAYS apply the MOST SPECIFIC protocol first, then supplement with more general protocols.
**Rule**: Safety-related protocols ALWAYS take priority over performance-related protocols. If safety and performance assessments conflict, safety wins.
**Rule**: The knowledge graph ensures NO assessment falls through the cracks. Every paper should be routed to at least one domain-specific protocol.

### AI-Generated Scientific Content Detection Protocol

AI-generated scientific content is increasingly infiltrating peer-reviewed literature. This protocol provides systematic detection and assessment:

| Content Type | Detection Signal | Detection Method | Severity | Action |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------|--------|
| **AI-Generated Text** | Unusual phrasing patterns; excessive hedging; formulaic structure; lack of domain-specific jargon errors (too perfect) | Text analysis: perplexity uniformity, vocabulary distribution, sentence length variance | HIGH | Flag: "⚠️ Suspected AI-generated text — verify claims independently" |
| **AI-Generated Figures** | Unnatural symmetry; inconsistent labeling; hallucinated data patterns; style inconsistency across figures | Visual inspection: axis label consistency, data distribution plausibility, figure style coherence | CRITICAL | Flag: "🔴 Suspected AI-generated figure — data may be fabricated" |
| **AI-Generated References** | Non-existent DOIs; journal name mismatch; author-name patterns; publication date inconsistencies | Reference verification: check DOI, PubMed ID, journal existence, author affiliation | CRITICAL | Flag: "🔴 Fabricated references detected — paper credibility compromised" |
| **AI-Generated Data** | Too-perfect distributions; implausible statistical properties; data that follows model predictions too closely | Statistical analysis: Benford's law, distribution normality, correlation structure plausibility | CRITICAL | Flag: "🔴 Suspected fabricated data — statistical properties implausible" |
| **AI-Assisted Peer Review** | Reviewer comments with AI-typical phrasing; lack of domain-specific critique; generic praise | Review analysis: specificity of comments, domain expertise signals, critique depth | HIGH | Flag: "⚠️ Suspected AI-assisted review — verify review quality" |

**Detection Decision Tree**:

```
Paper under evaluation
│
├─ Step 1: Reference Check (fastest, highest yield)
│   ├─ ALL references have valid DOIs/PubMed IDs? → Continue
│   └─ ANY reference cannot be verified? → 🔴 Flag: Fabricated reference(s)
│
├─ Step 2: Data Plausibility Check
│   ├─ Statistical properties consistent with known distributions? → Continue
│   ├─ Sample sizes and effect sizes consistent with domain norms? → Continue
│   └─ Data appears "too perfect" or "too consistent"? → 🟡 Flag: Suspected AI-generated data
│
├─ Step 3: Figure Integrity Check
│   ├─ Figures consistent with described methods? → Continue
│   ├─ Axis labels, units, scales correct and consistent? → Continue
│   └─ Unnatural patterns or inconsistencies? → 🟡 Flag: Suspected AI-generated figures
│
├─ Step 4: Text Analysis
│   ├─ Writing style consistent throughout? → Continue
│   ├─ Domain-specific terminology used correctly? → Continue
│   └─ Unusual phrasing or excessive uniformity? → 🟡 Flag: Suspected AI-generated text
│
└─ Step 5: Overall Assessment
    ├─ 0 flags → Standard assessment
    ├─ 1 🟡 flag → Enhanced scrutiny; verify key claims independently
    ├─ 2+ 🟡 flags → HIGH risk; treat all claims as unverified
    └─ ANY 🔴 flag → CRITICAL; paper credibility compromised; do not use as evidence
```

**Rule**: AI-generated scientific content is a GROWING THREAT to literature integrity. Detection capability must evolve as generation methods improve.
**Rule**: A paper with fabricated references is UNTRUSTWORTHY regardless of other qualities. One fabricated reference casts doubt on ALL references and ALL claims.
**Rule**: When AI-generated content is detected in a published paper, report to the journal and add to the Retraction Cascade Assessment if the paper is retracted.
**Rule**: The Bixonimania hoax (2024) demonstrated that AI can propagate fabricated medical information across multiple publications. This is a SYSTEMIC risk, not an isolated incident.

### Clinical AI Alert Fatigue Assessment Protocol

Alert fatigue from clinical AI systems is a major patient safety concern that undermines deployment effectiveness:

| Assessment Dimension | What to Measure | Red Flag Threshold | Clinical Impact |
|---------------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Alert Volume** | Number of AI alerts per clinician per shift | >50 alerts/shift OR >10 alerts/hour | Clinicians begin ignoring alerts; critical alerts missed |
| **Positive Predictive Value** | Fraction of alerts that are clinically actionable | PPV <10% | >90% of alerts are false positives; clinician trust eroded |
| **Alert-to-Action Ratio** | Fraction of alerts that lead to clinical action | <20% action rate | Most alerts are clinically irrelevant; workflow disruption without benefit |
| **Override Rate** | Fraction of AI recommendations overridden by clinicians | >80% override rate | Clinicians systematically disagree with AI; AI is not trusted |
| **Time-to-Dismiss** | Average time clinicians spend dismissing non-actionable alerts | >30 seconds per non-actionable alert | Cumulative time loss; workflow interruption |
| **Critical Alert Miss Rate** | Fraction of critical alerts missed due to fatigue | >5% of critical alerts missed | Direct patient harm risk |
| **Alert Escalation Effectiveness** | Whether escalated alerts receive appropriate attention | <50% of escalated alerts acted upon within 1 hour | Escalation system ineffective; high-severity alerts not differentiated |
| **Cognitive Load Impact** | Impact on clinician cognitive function during alert-heavy periods | Measurable decline in concurrent task performance | Patient safety compromise beyond AI's direct scope |

**Alert Fatigue Mitigation Assessment**:

| Mitigation Strategy | Description | Assessment Criteria | When to Apply |
|-------------------|-------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Tiered Alerting** | Different urgency levels with different interruption patterns | Are tiers clinically validated? Is tier assignment accurate? | Always; baseline requirement |
| **Context-Aware Suppression** | Suppress alerts when clinical context makes them irrelevant | Is suppression logic transparent? Can suppressed alerts be reviewed? | High-volume alert environments |
| **Batch Notification** | Group non-urgent alerts for periodic review | Batch interval clinically appropriate? Urgent alerts still real-time? | Moderate alert volume |
| **Adaptive Threshold** | Adjust alert thresholds based on clinical context and clinician preferences | Does adaptation maintain safety? Is it transparent? | Variable-risk clinical scenarios |
| **Confidence-Gated Alerting** | Only alert when AI confidence exceeds threshold | Is confidence well-calibrated? What happens at the threshold boundary? | All AI alert systems |

**Rule**: Alert fatigue is a PATIENT SAFETY issue, not a usability issue. A system that generates >50 non-actionable alerts per shift is DANGEROUS, regardless of its technical accuracy.
**Rule**: Epic Sepsis Model's alert fatigue (low sensitivity + high false positive rate at hundreds of hospitals) is a CASE STUDY in alert fatigue harm. Any clinical AI deployment must assess alert fatigue BEFORE and AFTER deployment.
**Rule**: If a clinical AI system's PPV <10%, it should NOT be deployed without significant redesign. The harm from alert fatigue outweighs the benefit of occasional correct alerts.
**Rule**: Alert fatigue assessment must include BOTH quantitative metrics (PPV, override rate) AND qualitative assessment (clinician interviews, workflow observation).

### AI-Human Team Performance Assessment Protocol

AI and human performance must be assessed as a TEAM, not just individually. This protocol evaluates team dynamics:

| Team Dimension | Assessment Method | Key Metric | Optimal Range | Red Flag |
|---------------|------------------|-----------|--------------|---------|
| **Team Synergy** | Compare: AI+Human vs max(AI alone, Human alone) | Synergy ratio = (AI+Human) / max(AI, Human) | >1.1 (team outperforms best individual) | <1.0 (team WORSE than best individual) |
| **Automation Bias** | Measure: agreement rate with AI when AI is wrong | Incorrect agreement rate | <10% | >30% (clinicians blindly follow AI) |
| **Skill Degradation** | Compare: human performance before AI vs after prolonged AI use | Performance delta on unassisted tasks | <5% decline | >15% decline (significant skill loss) |
| **Trust Calibration** | Measure: correlation between AI confidence and human trust | Trust-accuracy correlation | >0.7 (well-calibrated) | <0.3 (miscalibrated: over-trust or under-trust) |
| **Disagreement Resolution** | Analyze: how AI-human disagreements are resolved | Correct resolution rate | >70% correct | <50% correct (wrong side wins too often) |
| **Learning Effect** | Measure: whether human learns from AI over time | Improvement in unassisted performance over time | Positive trend | Negative trend (dependency without learning) |
| **Communication Efficiency** | Measure: time to reach decision with vs without AI | Decision time ratio | <1.5× (AI helps decide faster) | >3× (AI slows decision-making) |

**Team Performance Degradation Patterns**:

| Pattern | Description | Detection | Consequence | Mitigation |
|---------|-------------|-----------|------------|-----------|
| **Rubber-Stamping** | Human always agrees with AI regardless of correctness | Agreement rate >95% even when AI is experimentally wrong | Critical errors missed; AI failures amplified | Mandatory independent assessment before AI reveal |
| **Contrarian Override** | Human always overrides AI regardless of correctness | Override rate >80% even when AI is experimentally correct | AI value negated; potential missed diagnoses | Show AI confidence + evidence; require justification for override |
| **Skill Atrophy** | Human's independent capability declines with AI use | Unassisted performance declines >15% after 6+ months of AI use | Dangerous if AI becomes unavailable | Periodic unassisted practice; skill maintenance protocol |
| **Confidence Transfer** | Human adopts AI's confidence level regardless of own assessment | Human confidence correlates more with AI confidence than case difficulty | Overconfident on hard cases when AI is confident | Independent confidence rating before AI reveal |
